summary.value: 31-year-old male presenting with progressive fatigue, headaches, joint stiffness, and malar rash, ultimately diagnosed with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus after positive ANA, anti-dsDNA, and complement testing.

notes.value:
Patient presents with bilateral frontal headaches occurring 3-4 times per week for the past 3 weeks. Onset gradual, no photophobia or aura. Pain is dull, pressure-like, rated 5/10, partially relieved by OTC acetaminophen. No recent head trauma, vision changes, or neurological symptoms. Denies fever, weight loss, or night sweats.

Exam unremarkable. Neurological exam normal. Started ibuprofen 400mg PRN for headache relief. Ordered basic labs (CBC, CMP, TSH) to rule out systemic causes. Return in 1 week for lab review.

Patient returns for review of basic labs ordered at initial visit. CBC within normal limits, CMP normal, TSH 2.1 (normal). Headaches continue at similar frequency but are manageable with ibuprofen.

No red flag symptoms identified. Headaches attributed to tension-type given bilateral location, pressure quality, and normal labs. Advised stress management, adequate hydration, and regular sleep schedule. Continue ibuprofen PRN. Follow up as needed or if new symptoms develop.

Patient returns reporting headaches have persisted over the summer despite ibuprofen use. New complaint of significant fatigue and low energy over the past 3-4 weeks, requiring afternoon naps. No weight change, no fever, no sleep disturbance beyond the fatigue itself.

Expanded labs reveal Vitamin D level of 18 ng/mL (deficient). Remainder of labs unremarkable. Started Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily supplementation. Fatigue may be multifactorial — Vitamin D deficiency likely contributing. Will reassess in 2-3 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Fatigue persists, new morning joint stiffness

notes.value:
Patient reports fatigue has not improved despite 2.5 weeks on Vitamin D supplementation. New symptom: morning stiffness in bilateral hands lasting approximately 45 minutes, improving with activity. No joint swelling or redness noted on exam. No rash, oral ulcers, or hair loss.

Given new inflammatory symptom, ordered ESR and CRP. ESR mildly elevated at 28 mm/hr (ref 0-20). CRP borderline at 0.8 mg/dL (ref <1.0). Mild systemic inflammation noted — monitoring closely. Advised to track joint symptoms and return if worsening.

chief_complaint.value: Worsening joint stiffness, intermittent low-grade fever

notes.value:
Patient reports morning stiffness has progressed to involve bilateral wrists and knees in addition to hands. Duration now exceeding 1 hour. Intermittent low-grade fever (measured at home: 99.8F) over the past week. Reports occasional mouth sores that resolve spontaneously. Headaches continue.

Detailed family history elicited: mother diagnosed with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus at age 35, maternal aunt with Rheumatoid Arthritis diagnosed at age 42. This family history of autoimmune disease, combined with progressive inflammatory symptoms, raises concern for a systemic autoimmune process.

Ordered ANA panel and repeat inflammatory markers. Counseled patient on the differential and the importance of further workup. Will call with results.

chief_complaint.value: Lab review — ANA results, new facial rash

notes.value:
Patient called in for urgent lab review. ANA returned strongly positive at 1:320 titer (speckled pattern). ESR has risen to 45 mm/hr. CBC shows new pancytopenia: WBC 3.2, Hgb 11.8, Plt 135 — all below normal range.

On exam today, patient has developed a new erythematous rash across bilateral malar eminences sparing the nasolabial folds, consistent with a malar (butterfly) rash. No discoid lesions. Mild bilateral wrist synovitis palpable.

Ibuprofen discontinued due to concern for renal effects in setting of suspected autoimmune disease. Urgent rheumatology referral placed. Referral letter prepared. Patient counseled on suspected lupus diagnosis and the need for confirmatory testing by rheumatology.

Formal referral letter prepared summarizing clinical course for rheumatology consultation. Letter documents the 4-month progression from tension-type headaches through fatigue, joint stiffness, elevated inflammatory markers, positive ANA, pancytopenia, and malar rash. Family history of SLE in mother and RA in maternal aunt highlighted as significant risk factor.

Key labs summarized: ANA 1:320, ESR 45, WBC 3.2, Hgb 11.8, Plt 135. Patient aware of referral and anticipating rheumatology appointment within the next 1-2 weeks.

X-ray of bilateral hands and wrists obtained to evaluate for erosive arthropathy. Results show no erosive changes or joint space narrowing. Mild soft tissue swelling noted around bilateral MCP and wrist joints, consistent with inflammatory arthropathy without structural damage.

Findings are reassuring for absence of erosive disease. Results consistent with early inflammatory process rather than established destructive arthritis. Will share imaging with rheumatology for their upcoming consult.

outcome.value: No erosive changes, mild soft tissue swelling consistent with inflammatory arthropathy

notes.value:
New patient rheumatology consultation for suspected Systemic Lupus Erythematosus. Reviewed PCP records documenting 4-month progression of headaches, fatigue, polyarthralgia, pancytopenia, positive ANA 1:320, and malar rash. Significant family history: mother with SLE, maternal aunt with RA.

Confirmatory serologies obtained: Anti-dsDNA antibody 85 IU/mL (elevated, ref <30), C3 65 mg/dL (low, ref 90-180), C4 8 mg/dL (low, ref 10-40), Anti-Smith antibody positive. Patient meets 4+ ACR classification criteria for SLE: malar rash, arthritis, hematologic disorder (pancytopenia), and positive ANA with confirmatory anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith.

Diagnosis: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus. Initiated hydroxychloroquine 200mg twice daily as maintenance therapy, and prednisone 20mg daily with planned taper over 8 weeks as bridging anti-inflammatory. Baseline ophthalmology referral recommended for hydroxychloroquine monitoring. Labs to be rechecked in 3 weeks.

Dermatology consultation for evaluation of malar rash in setting of newly diagnosed SLE. Examination reveals well-demarcated erythematous patches over bilateral malar eminences with sparing of nasolabial folds, consistent with acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus. No discoid lesions, no photosensitivity-related changes elsewhere.

Skin biopsy performed from right malar region. Histopathology shows interface dermatitis with vacuolar changes at the dermoepidermal junction. Direct immunofluorescence (lupus band test) positive, showing granular deposition of IgG and C3 at the basement membrane zone.

Findings confirm acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus. Recommend sun protection, topical corticosteroids for flare management, and continued systemic treatment per rheumatology. Follow-up as needed for skin symptoms.

outcome.value: Lupus band test positive, consistent with acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus

notes.value:
Follow-up visit 3 weeks after SLE diagnosis and treatment initiation. Patient reports improvement in joint pain and morning stiffness — stiffness duration reduced from >1 hour to ~20 minutes. Fatigue moderately improved. Malar rash fading. No new symptoms. Tolerating hydroxychloroquine and prednisone without side effects.

Prednisone taper in progress: currently at 15mg daily, plan to reduce by 5mg every 2 weeks. Repeat labs show anti-dsDNA trending down to 65 IU/mL. CBC stabilizing: WBC 3.8, Hgb 12.2, Plt 148. Urinalysis normal with no proteinuria — no evidence of lupus nephritis at this time.

Continue current regimen. Recheck labs in 2-3 weeks. Follow up in 6 weeks to assess prednisone taper completion.

PCP follow-up to coordinate care with rheumatology and dermatology. Patient reports overall improvement since starting SLE treatment. Fatigue significantly better. Headaches have resolved entirely — supports retrospective attribution to systemic inflammation rather than primary tension headache. Joint stiffness minimal.

Vitamin D rechecked at 32 ng/mL (improved from 18, now within normal range). Will continue supplementation at current dose. Coordinating with Dr. Torres regarding prednisone taper timeline and hydroxychloroquine monitoring schedule.

Follow-up visit after completion of 8-week prednisone taper. Patient has been off prednisone for 5 days. No symptom rebound — joint pain remains well controlled, malar rash fully resolved, energy level good. Tolerating hydroxychloroquine well with no GI side effects.

Labs show continued improvement: ESR 22 (near normal), C3 improving at 82 (still mildly low), C4 normalized at 12, CBC normalizing with WBC 4.2, Hgb 12.8, Plt 165. Prednisone officially discontinued. Plan: continue hydroxychloroquine maintenance. Quarterly follow-up with labs. Annual ophthalmology screening for hydroxychloroquine retinal toxicity.

Routine PCP follow-up, approximately 3 months since SLE diagnosis. Patient doing well overall. No flare symptoms — no joint pain, rash, fever, or oral ulcers. Energy level has returned to near-baseline. Headaches have not recurred.

Review of systems notable for mild dry eyes, which may be medication-related or early sicca symptoms — will monitor. Blood pressure normal. Weight stable. Reinforced importance of sun protection, medication adherence, and prompt reporting of any new symptoms (especially foamy urine, chest pain, or worsening fatigue). Labs deferred to upcoming rheumatology visit next week.

First quarterly rheumatology follow-up since prednisone discontinuation. Patient clinically stable on hydroxychloroquine monotherapy. No flare symptoms. SLEDAI score 2 (serologic activity only). Physical exam unremarkable — no active synovitis, no rash, no lymphadenopathy.

Labs: Anti-dsDNA trending down to 42 IU/mL (still mildly elevated but improving). C3 88 (approaching normal). C4 15 (normal). CBC fully normalized: WBC 5.1, Hgb 13.5, Plt 195. Urinalysis clear.

Plan: continue hydroxychloroquine 200mg BID. Annual ophthalmology screening scheduled. Next rheumatology visit in 3 months. Patient to call if any flare symptoms develop between visits.

summary.value: 58-year-old female with long-standing Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and Essential Hypertension, well-controlled on Metformin, Lisinopril, Aspirin, and Simvastatin through consistent follow-up with primary care and endocrinology.

chief_complaint.value: Establish primary care, routine diabetes and hypertension management

notes.value:
Patient Maria Garcia, a 58-year-old female, establishes care with Dr. Rodriguez today. She reports long-standing diagnoses of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and Essential Hypertension, both previously managed by a physician in another city. Her current medications are Metformin 500mg BID and Lisinopril 10mg QD. She reports no acute complaints but expresses desire for proactive management of her chronic conditions. She checks her blood sugar intermittently at home, generally reporting values in the 150-200 mg/dL range. Blood pressure today is 148/92 mmHg.

Physical exam reveals no acute distress. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable. No peripheral edema. Ordered comprehensive baseline labs including HbA1c, full lipid panel, and kidney function panel to assess current disease control and organ function. Reinforced the importance of diet and exercise for chronic disease management. Will follow up in two weeks for lab review.

chief_complaint.value: Lab review, diabetes and hypertension follow-up

notes.value:
Patient returns for review of labs ordered at initial visit. Results indicate suboptimal control of both diabetes and hypertension. HbA1c is 8.0%, fasting glucose 168 mg/dL. Lipid panel shows elevated LDL 135 mg/dL and triglycerides 210 mg/dL. Kidney function panel is within normal limits, with estimated GFR >60 mL/min/1.73m². Blood pressure today remains elevated at 145/90 mmHg.

Discussed the implications of these lab results with the patient, emphasizing the need for stricter control to prevent long-term complications. Reviewed current dietary habits and encouraged increased physical activity. Due to persistent elevated HbA1c, a referral to endocrinology was initiated to optimize her diabetes regimen. Referral letter provided. Patient encouraged to continue current medications and lifestyle modifications.

chief_complaint.value: Endocrinology consult for uncontrolled diabetes and dyslipidemia

notes.value:
Patient seen for initial endocrinology consultation following referral from Dr. Rodriguez. Reviewed medical history, including long-standing T2DM and HTN. Patient reports adherence to Metformin 500mg BID and Lisinopril 10mg QD, but notes difficulty with consistent dietary changes. Home blood glucose readings average around 150-180 mg/dL. Current HbA1c is 8.0% (from prior PCP visit).

Assessment: Uncontrolled Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and Essential Hypertension. Plan: Increased Metformin dosage to 1000mg twice daily to improve glycemic control. Advised initiating low-dose aspirin (81mg daily) for primary cardiovascular prevention given her age, diabetes, and hypertension. Patient given prescription for Aspirin. Will follow up in 6 weeks to re-evaluate glycemic control and discuss further management.

chief_complaint.value: Endocrinology follow-up, diabetes and dyslipidemia management

notes.value:
Patient returns for follow-up with endocrinology. Reports improved energy levels and slightly lower home blood glucose readings since increasing Metformin to 1000mg BID. She is also taking Aspirin 81mg daily. Blood pressure today is 138/85 mmHg. Repeat HbA1c is 7.5%, showing good response to increased Metformin. However, lipid panel still indicates dyslipidemia with LDL 128 mg/dL.

Given persistent dyslipidemia and overall cardiovascular risk profile, initiated Simvastatin 20mg daily. Patient educated on potential side effects and the importance of adherence. Reviewed diet and exercise goals. Emphasized consistency with home blood pressure monitoring. Plan to recheck labs, including lipid panel and HbA1c, in 2 months. Follow up in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: Routine PCP follow-up, general health check

notes.value:
Patient presents for routine follow-up with her primary care physician. She reports feeling much better overall, with significantly improved fatigue and no new concerns. Blood pressure readings at home are consistently around 125-135/75-85 mmHg. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, or any new neurological symptoms. Medications are well-tolerated.

Physical exam today is unremarkable. Blood pressure 128/80 mmHg. Discussed continued adherence to medications and lifestyle recommendations. Labs ordered for HbA1c and kidney function to monitor the effects of current management. Patient to follow up with endocrinology next, and then return to PCP in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: Endocrinology follow-up, stable diabetes and dyslipidemia

notes.value:
Patient returns to endocrinology for routine follow-up. Reports good glycemic control with home blood sugars mostly below 140 mg/dL. No hypoglycemic episodes. Continues to take Metformin 1000mg BID, Lisinopril 10mg QD, Aspirin 81mg QD, and Simvastatin 20mg QD. Blood pressure today is 128/78 mmHg. Repeat HbA1c is 6.8%, indicating excellent control. Lipid panel shows LDL 95 mg/dL and triglycerides 140 mg/dL, both within target ranges.

Assessment: Well-controlled Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Essential Hypertension, and Dyslipidemia. Encouraged continued adherence to medications and healthy lifestyle. No changes to medication regimen at this time. Plan to follow up in 4-5 months. Continue annual eye and foot exams.

chief_complaint.value: Annual physical and chronic condition review

notes.value:
Patient presents for her annual physical examination and review of chronic conditions. She continues to feel well, maintaining good control of her diabetes and hypertension. She reports consistent adherence to all medications. Home blood pressure readings remain within target, and she has experienced no diabetic complications or hypertensive symptoms. No new concerns.

Physical exam is stable. Blood pressure 125/75 mmHg. Heart and lung exams are normal. No new findings. Reviewed all current medications and confirmed dosages. Discussed the importance of upcoming annual screenings, including mammogram and colonoscopy. Encouraged flu vaccination. Overall, patient continues to manage her chronic conditions effectively. Continue current plan.

summary.value: 45-year-old male presented with acute chest pain, diagnosed with ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI), successfully treated with percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) and stent placement, now stable on medical therapy and undergoing cardiac rehabilitation.

chief_complaint.value: Acute onset severe chest pain radiating to left arm

notes.value: Patient presented to the Emergency Department via EMS with 9/10 substernal chest pain, sudden onset 1 hour prior. Associated with shortness of breath, diaphoresis, and left arm radiation. ECG performed showing ST-segment elevations in leads V2-V4. Troponin I was critically elevated at 8.2 ng/mL. Rapid activation of cardiac catheterization lab. Patient received aspirin 325mg chewable, clopidogrel 300mg loading dose, and morphine for pain. Suspected anterior STEMI. Transferred to cath lab for emergent PCI.

outcome.value: ST-segment elevation in V2-V4, consistent with anterior STEMI
Successful placement of drug-eluting stent in LAD artery, TIMI 3 flow achieved

notes.value: Patient is two days post-successful PCI to LAD. Chest pain has completely resolved. Hemodynamically stable. Peripheral pulses intact. No signs of bleeding at access site. Reviewed post-PCI care, including importance of dual antiplatelet therapy adherence. Troponin levels are trending down. Patient ambulating in the hallways with assistance. Tolerating oral intake well. Discussed cardiac rehabilitation referral. Morphine discontinued as acute pain has resolved.

chief_complaint.value: Discharge from hospital post-STEMI and PCI

notes.value: Patient discharged home in stable condition. Final instructions for medication adherence (Aspirin, Clopidogrel, Atorvastatin, Metoprolol, Nitroglycerin PRN), lifestyle modifications (diet, exercise, smoking cessation counseling), and symptom recognition (return precautions for chest pain). Follow-up scheduled with cardiology in 3 weeks and PCP in 1 month. Cardiac rehabilitation referral made. Patient verbalized understanding of discharge plan.

Patient returns for a one-month post-STEMI follow-up. Reports no recurrent chest pain, shortness of breath, or other cardiac symptoms. Tolerating all medications well, no adverse effects reported. Adhering to diet recommendations and has started light walking. Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, heart rate 62 bpm. Re-emphasized importance of medication adherence, especially DAPT. Encouraged continued participation in cardiac rehab. Labs reviewed, showing good lipid control.

Patient attended initial cardiac rehabilitation consult. Reviewed medical history, recent STEMI, and PCI. Assessed current physical activity level and discussed goals for rehabilitation. Patient is motivated to improve fitness and reduce cardiac risk factors. Baseline exercise capacity assessed. Initiated Phase 2 cardiac rehabilitation program, including supervised exercise sessions, education on heart-healthy living, and stress management techniques. Provided individualized exercise prescription. Next session scheduled for next week.

outcome.value: Good baseline tolerance, mild deconditioning, safe to proceed with moderate intensity exercise program.

notes.value: Patient seen for routine follow-up with his primary care physician, approximately 4 months post-STEMI. Reports feeling much better, actively participating in cardiac rehab 3 times a week. Has lost 10 lbs through diet and exercise. Blood pressure consistently in normal range (120-130/70-80 mmHg) at home. No new concerns. Medications are well-tolerated. Reviewed diabetes screening labs, HbA1c is normal. Renal function stable. Encouraged continued progress in cardiac rehab and adherence to all medication regimens.

Patient returns for his 6-month post-STEMI cardiology follow-up. Has completed his cardiac rehabilitation program with significant improvement in exercise tolerance and overall well-being. Continues to maintain healthy lifestyle modifications. No cardiac symptoms. Blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, heart rate 60 bpm. Discussed continuation of DAPT (Aspirin and Clopidogrel) for at least 12 months as per guidelines, with a plan to re-evaluate at 1 year. Repeat lipid panel shows excellent control. Plan for next cardiology follow-up in 6 months for ongoing surveillance.

summary.value: 27-year-old female presenting with chronic fatigue and pallor, diagnosed with Iron Deficiency Anemia after laboratory confirmation. Initiated on oral iron supplementation with symptomatic improvement.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent fatigue and weakness for several months

notes.value:
Patient presents with a 6-month history of worsening fatigue, generalized weakness, and occasional dizziness. Reports feeling constantly tired, impacting daily activities and work performance. Denies fever, weight loss, or changes in bowel habits. Reports heavy menstrual periods which may contribute to blood loss.

Physical exam reveals conjunctival pallor and pale nail beds. Heart rate 88 bpm, regular. Lungs clear to auscultation. No lymphadenopathy. Ordered CBC, serum iron panel (iron, ferritin, TIBC). Started empirical Ferrous Sulfate 325mg daily, pending lab results. Advised to increase dietary iron intake.

Patient returns for review of labs. CBC shows microcytic hypochromic anemia (Hgb 9.5 g/dL, MCV 72 fL). Iron panel reveals significantly low ferritin (8 ng/mL) and low serum iron (25 mcg/dL) with elevated TIBC (450 mcg/dL), confirming iron deficiency anemia.

Diagnosis of Iron Deficiency Anemia confirmed. Discussed probable etiology related to heavy menstrual bleeding. Reinforced importance of consistent Ferrous Sulfate use. Advised to take iron with Vitamin C and away from dairy/calcium. Educated on symptoms of improvement and potential side effects. Will recheck labs in 8-10 weeks.

Patient returns for a routine check-up, approximately 12 weeks after starting iron supplementation. Reports significant improvement in fatigue and energy levels. No longer experiencing dizziness. Menstrual bleeding still heavy but has not worsened.

Physical exam shows improved conjunctival color. Repeat labs demonstrate good response to therapy: Hemoglobin increased to 12.8 g/dL, Ferritin to 45 ng/mL. Iron stores are replenishing, and anemia is resolving. Advised to continue Ferrous Sulfate for another 3-4 months to fully replete iron stores and then consider reducing frequency or discontinuing based on future lab results. Referred to gynecology for evaluation of heavy menstrual bleeding.

summary.value: 72-year-old male with severe Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), on home oxygen, presenting for routine follow-ups following a history of recurrent pneumonia.

chief_complaint.value: Establish care for severe COPD, review current management

notes.value:
Patient is a 72-year-old male with a long history of severe COPD, primarily emphysema type, with significant dyspnea on exertion. He is on 2L home oxygen continuously. He reports generally stable symptoms but recalls his last pneumonia exacerbation 3 months ago. He is compliant with his current inhaler regimen of Tiotropium and Albuterol PRN. He smokes 1/2 pack per day and has a 50 pack-year history. 

Physical exam reveals barrel chest, distant breath sounds, and mild wheezing. Oxygen saturation is 92% on 2L nasal cannula. Reviewed medication adherence and emphasized smoking cessation. Confirmed COPD diagnosis and current home oxygen use. Ordered baseline labs and chest X-ray. Scheduled follow-up.

chief_complaint.value: Routine follow-up, review labs and chest X-ray

notes.value:
Patient returns for follow-up. Reports no acute exacerbations since last visit. Continues to use home oxygen regularly and manages dyspnea with Albuterol PRN. He admits to occasional smoking but states he is trying to cut down. 

Review of chest X-ray shows hyperinflation and flattened diaphragms consistent with emphysema, no acute infiltrates. Labs: CBC within normal limits, electrolytes normal. Oxygen saturation today is 93% on 2L NC. Discussed importance of flu and pneumonia vaccinations. Continue current medications. Reinforced smoking cessation counseling. Scheduled next routine follow-up.

outcome.value: Findings consistent with emphysema, no acute infiltrates

notes.value:
Patient presents for annual COPD check-up. Reports good stability over the past few months, no hospitalizations or emergency visits. Denies worsening cough, shortness of breath, or fever. He received his flu shot last fall but has not yet received the latest pneumonia vaccine. 

Physical exam stable. Lung sounds remain distant but clear, no new wheezing. Oxygen saturation 94% on 2L NC. Discussed and administered pneumococcal vaccine today. Emphasized continued smoking cessation efforts and adherence to home oxygen therapy. Advised to continue current bronchodilator regimen. Return in 6 months or sooner for any acute concerns.

outcome.value: Administered successfully

summary.value: A 28-year-old park ranger presenting with escalating neuropsychiatric symptoms over 9 months, initially misdiagnosed as stress-related depression, eventually identified as Solanum-type encephalopathy (Zombie Virus) by the CDC.

condition_012.label: Adjustment Disorder with Mixed Anxiety and Depressed Mood

name.value: Adjustment Disorder with Mixed Anxiety and Depressed Mood

medication_024.label: Experimental Antiviral XR-7 IV per CDC protocol

notes.value:
Marcus Reed, a 28-year-old park ranger, presents with a 3-week history of persistent fatigue, irritability, and insomnia. He attributes his symptoms to increased work stress during spring wildlife season. He denies substance use, recent illness, or trauma.

Vitals are within normal limits. Physical exam is unremarkable. Patient appears tired but cooperative. Sleep hygiene counseling provided. Basic labs ordered to rule out metabolic causes.

Patient referred by Dr. Patel for evaluation of worsening mood and irritability. PHQ-9 score is 14 (moderate depression). Patient reports feeling constantly 'on edge,' difficulty concentrating at work, and increasing irritability with coworkers.

He denies suicidal ideation, psychotic symptoms, or substance use. Sleep remains disrupted with early morning awakening. Assessment: Adjustment disorder with mixed anxiety and depressed mood in the context of occupational stress. Initiating sertraline 50mg daily.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent fatigue, mild fever, animal scratch on forearm

notes.value:
Patient returns reporting persistent fatigue despite sertraline. New complaint: a healing scratch on the right forearm from an 'unknown animal encounter' approximately 3 weeks ago during a night patrol. He did not see the animal clearly. The wound is inflamed but partially healed.

Temp 99.4°F, otherwise vitals normal. Wound shows surrounding erythema with serous discharge. Basic wound care provided. CBC shows mild leukocytosis (WBC 11.2). CRP mildly elevated at 1.8 mg/dL. Advised to return if wound worsens or fever increases.

Patient reports escalating outbursts of anger, difficulty concentrating, and persistent poor sleep despite sertraline. He describes episodes where he 'loses control' and shouts at coworkers unprovoked. Sertraline does not appear to be helping.

Patient appears unusually agitated during the visit, pacing the exam room and clenching fists. No psychotic features elicited. Adding lorazepam 1mg PRN for acute agitation. Will monitor closely.

New and concerning symptoms: episodes of confusion with disorientation to place, excessive salivation, and mild dysphagia when drinking water. Patient reports the forearm wound has worsened with spreading erythema despite prior treatment.

Temp 100.2°F. Wound on right forearm shows expanding erythema and early eschar formation. Neurological exam notable for mild facial asymmetry and hyperactive gag reflex. Metabolic panel and liver function ordered. CRP significantly elevated at 3.2 mg/dL. Referring to neurology urgently given behavioral changes, confusion, and new neurological findings.

chief_complaint.value: Neurology consult for behavioral changes and confusion

notes.value:
Referred by Dr. Patel for evaluation of progressive behavioral changes, confusion, hypersalivation, and new neurological signs. Exam reveals hyperreflexia in all extremities, mild nuchal rigidity, and an exaggerated startle response. Patient is intermittently oriented.

MRI brain shows subtle T2 hyperintensities in bilateral temporal lobes. Lumbar puncture performed: CSF shows mild pleocytosis (WBC 45), elevated protein (68 mg/dL), and normal glucose. No organisms on gram stain. Rabies IFA testing ordered given animal bite history and clinical presentation. Impression: encephalitis of unclear etiology.

outcome.value: CSF: mild pleocytosis (WBC 45), elevated protein (68 mg/dL), normal glucose. No organisms on gram stain.

chief_complaint.value: MRI brain review and medication adjustment

notes.value:
Formal MRI interpretation: subtle T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in bilateral mesial temporal lobes and insular cortex. No mass effect, no gadolinium enhancement. The pattern is not consistent with HSV encephalitis (no hemorrhagic component) or typical autoimmune encephalitis.

Given the atypical imaging and clinical picture, sertraline is being discontinued per neurology recommendation to reduce pharmacological confounders. Awaiting rabies IFA, prion markers, and HSV PCR results.

Critical lab results reviewed: Rabies IFA negative, 14-3-3 protein negative, RT-QuIC negative, HSV PCR not detected. All standard infectious and prion etiologies have been excluded.

EEG shows unusual generalized slowing with periodic discharges that do not match any known epileptiform pattern or recognized encephalopathy signature. Patient continues to deteriorate with worsening confusion, intermittent aggression, and increasing difficulty with oral intake. The clinical picture remains unexplained by all standard workups.

Patient brought to ED after attacking nursing staff during a scheduled follow-up visit. Patient lunged at and bit a nurse on the forearm, appearing to attempt to consume tissue. Displayed no pain response when physically restrained. Severe photophobia noted — patient recoiled from overhead lighting. Hydrophobia also observed. Temp 101.4°F. Patient is combative, vocalizing incoherently, with excessive salivation.

Restraints applied per protocol. Physical exam notable for dilated pupils, tachycardia (HR 118), and the expanding necrotic wound on the right forearm. Started empiric acyclovir 10mg/kg IV and dexamethasone 4mg IV for presumed viral encephalitis. Haloperidol 5mg IM administered for acute agitation. Lorazepam discontinued in favor of haloperidol given the severity of episodes. Infectious disease consult requested urgently.

chief_complaint.value: Infectious disease consult for undiagnosed encephalitis

notes.value:
Comprehensive infectious workup initiated. West Nile IgM negative, EEE negative, HIV non-reactive, RPR non-reactive, QuantiFERON negative. Repeat lumbar puncture performed: CSF shows worsening pleocytosis (WBC 120), protein elevated to 95 mg/dL, and novel protein bands on electrophoresis that do not match any reference database.

Notably, a necrotic skin lesion has developed at the original animal bite wound site on the right forearm. Wound biopsy performed: necrotic tissue with unusual intracellular inclusions, no identifiable organism on standard staining. Acyclovir discontinued given no viral etiology identified. Levetiracetam 500mg BID initiated for seizure prophylaxis. Specimens sent to state laboratory for advanced characterization.

outcome.value: Necrotic tissue with unusual intracellular inclusions, no identifiable organism on standard staining

notes.value:
Continuous EEG monitoring reveals a novel periodic pattern with 2-3Hz generalized discharges that do not match any catalogued encephalopathy signature in the EEG atlas. The pattern is distinct from CJD, NMDA receptor encephalitis, and all recognized periodic patterns.

Patient is increasingly non-verbal with intermittent violent episodes requiring physical restraint. Shuffling, uncoordinated gait observed when patient ambulates. Speech has been replaced by involuntary groaning vocalizations. Patient is increasingly non-verbal. GCS fluctuating between 9 and 12. Pupillary responses sluggish bilaterally. The clinical trajectory continues to worsen despite supportive care.

State laboratory results received: unable to classify the pathogen using standard microbiological, virological, and molecular methods. Novel viral-like particles identified on electron microscopy of the CSF specimen — approximately 80-100nm, icosahedral morphology, not matching any known viral family.

Wound biopsy review confirms necrotic tissue with intracellular inclusions not matching known pathogens. Samples have been forwarded to the CDC for advanced characterization. Enhanced isolation precautions maintained.

CDC confirms the presence of a novel pathogen not found in any existing viral, bacterial, or prion database. Preliminary characterization indicates a neurotropic RNA agent with a unique transmission profile and tropism for mesial temporal structures.

Internal designation is pending formal classification. Enhanced BSL-3 isolation protocols have been recommended by the CDC for all specimens and patient contact. The bitten nurse from the September incident is under active surveillance with no symptoms to date.

CDC internal advisory received with formal classification: Solanum-type encephalopathy (Zombie Virus). Three similar cases have been identified across North America, all with a history of animal bite exposure in rural or wilderness areas.

Transmission is suspected via bite vector, with high pathogen titers found in saliva. There is no known effective treatment at this time. Experimental antiviral protocols are under development at the CDC. Patient's condition continues to deteriorate with GCS now 8-9.

chief_complaint.value: CDC advisory document and epidemiological summary

notes.value:
Formal CDC advisory document prepared by Dr. Diaz. Classification: Solanum-type encephalopathy (Zombie Virus). Epidemiology: 3 confirmed cases in North America, all linked to unidentified animal bite exposure in wilderness settings.

Clinical progression follows a 3-phase pattern: prodromal (fatigue, irritability, mood changes), neuropsychiatric (aggression, confusion, hypersalivation, photophobia), and terminal (persistent altered consciousness, necrotic lesions, autonomic instability). Laboratory hallmarks include novel CSF protein bands, 2-3Hz periodic EEG pattern, and viral-like particles on electron microscopy. BSL-3 containment protocols required for all specimen handling.

chief_complaint.value: Neurology status update, experimental treatment initiation

notes.value:
Patient remains in a persistent altered state with GCS 8. Continued periodic EEG discharges unchanged. Necrotic lesions on the right forearm are stable and not expanding. Autonomic instability with intermittent tachycardia and blood pressure lability. Patient displays grayish pallor with sunken periorbital features. Necrotic lesions at original bite site have expanded. Despite diminished cognition (GCS 8), patient exhibits paradoxical approach behavior toward staff members, requiring continuous restraint. Involuntary groaning vocalizations persist.

CBC shows worsening leukocytosis (WBC 14.8) and new thrombocytopenia (Plt 98). Hemoglobin has dropped to 11.2. CRP markedly elevated at 8.5 mg/dL. An experimental antiviral under the CDC compassionate use protocol has been initiated. Prognosis remains guarded.

summary.value: 64-year-old female with Type 2 Diabetes presenting with progressive Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 4, complicated by secondary hyperparathyroidism and anemia of chronic disease, currently preparing for dialysis.

notes.value:
Patient Elena Vasquez, a 64-year-old female with known Type 2 Diabetes and Hypertension, presents with a 3-month history of progressive fatigue and bilateral ankle swelling. Denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or fever. Diabetes currently managed with Metformin. Blood pressure has been well-controlled per home readings. Exam shows 1+ pitting edema bilaterally in ankles. Labs ordered to assess renal function and other contributing factors.

Reviewed current medications, including Metformin and Lisinopril. Emphasized adherence and monitoring of blood pressure. Advised elevation of legs and compression socks for swelling. Follow-up for lab review.

Patient returns for lab review. Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL, eGFR 28 mL/min/1.73m2, indicating CKD Stage 4. Glucose 145 mg/dL, A1c 7.8%. Electrolytes otherwise normal. Urine microalbumin-to-creatinine ratio elevated. These findings suggest progressive diabetic nephropathy.

Discussed implications of CKD Stage 4 and the need for specialist input. Provided initial dietary counseling (low sodium, low protein). Urgent referral to Nephrology for comprehensive management and evaluation. Continued Metformin at reduced dose, Lisinopril as prescribed. Follow-up after nephrology consult.

Initial nephrology consultation for new CKD Stage 4. Reviewed patient's history of Type 2 Diabetes and Hypertension, and recent decline in GFR. Discussed diet modifications (low potassium, low phosphorus, moderate protein), fluid management, and importance of strict blood pressure and glucose control to slow CKD progression.

Physical exam unremarkable aside from mild ankle edema. Blood pressure 145/88 mmHg. Added Amlodipine 5mg daily to Lisinopril to achieve target BP of <130/80 mmHg. Ordered comprehensive renal panel, PTH, Vitamin D levels. Educated patient on signs of worsening renal function and potential future need for renal replacement therapy. Follow-up in 4 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Nephrology follow-up - BP control, lab review

notes.value:
Patient returns for follow-up. Reports feeling slightly better, ankle swelling reduced. Blood pressure improved at home. Labs show Creatinine 2.2 mg/dL, eGFR 27 mL/min/1.73m2, stable. Phosphorus 3.8 mg/dL, Calcium 9.0 mg/dL. PTH 85 pg/mL (mildly elevated). Vitamin D 25-OH 22 ng/mL (deficient).

Discussed importance of Vitamin D repletion, started D3 supplementation. Continued current antihypertensive regimen. Reviewed diet. Encouraged regular exercise. Scheduled next follow-up in 2 months. Reviewed importance of avoiding NSAIDs and contrast dyes.

chief_complaint.value: Routine diabetes and general health check

notes.value:
Routine check-up with PCP. Patient reports stable energy levels and reduced edema. Continues with nephrology care. Blood pressure 135/82 mmHg. A1c 7.9% (stable but not at goal). Fasting glucose 138 mg/dL. No new symptoms. Discussed importance of ongoing dietary adherence and medication compliance for diabetes management, especially with CKD.

Encouraged patient to continue working closely with nephrologist. Renewed prescriptions. Follow-up in 3 months for repeat A1c and general check-up.

Patient returns for follow-up. Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL, eGFR 25 mL/min/1.73m2. Phosphorus 5.0 mg/dL (elevated). Calcium 8.8 mg/dL. PTH 110 pg/mL (further elevated). Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL (mild anemia).

Initiated Sevelamer Carbonate 800mg with meals to control hyperphosphatemia. Discussed importance of limiting dietary phosphorus. Increased Vitamin D3 supplementation. Patient advised to continue close monitoring of blood pressure and glucose. Referred to renal dietitian for further dietary education. Follow-up in 6 weeks to recheck labs.

Follow-up visit. Phosphorus is better controlled at 4.6 mg/dL with sevelamer. However, PTH has risen to 140 pg/mL. Calcium remains stable at 8.9 mg/dL. This is consistent with worsening secondary hyperparathyroidism.

Initiated discussion on calcitriol therapy. Given the complexity of managing PTH, calcium, and phosphorus in CKD, decided to refer to Endocrinology for co-management of secondary hyperparathyroidism and optimization of diabetes control in the setting of advanced CKD. Continue current medications. Follow-up in 1 month.

Endocrinology consult for management of secondary hyperparathyroidism and Type 2 Diabetes in CKD Stage 4. Reviewed nephrology notes. Patient reports understanding of dietary restrictions. A1c 8.1% (suboptimal).

Initiated Calcitriol 0.25mcg daily to address elevated PTH. Reviewed Metformin dosing in CKD; maintained current dose given eGFR. Discussed options for better glycemic control, including GLP-1 agonists or basal insulin, but postponed until PTH and phosphorus are more stable. Emphasized monitoring calcium and phosphorus carefully while on calcitriol. Follow-up with labs in 1 month.

Follow-up visit. Creatinine 2.5 mg/dL, eGFR 24 mL/min/1.73m2. Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (worsening anemia). Iron studies (Ferritin 150 ng/mL, Transferrin Saturation 28%) indicate functional iron deficiency despite adequate stores due to inflammation. PTH slightly improved to 125 pg/mL with calcitriol.

Diagnosis of Anemia of Chronic Disease. Discussed initiating Erythropoietin Stimulating Agents (ESA). Ordered additional iron panel to confirm. Provided patient education on ESA therapy, potential side effects, and administration. Plan to start ESA after iron status confirmation. Follow-up in 3 weeks.

Patient returns to initiate Epoetin Alfa. Iron stores deemed adequate for ESA initiation. Prescribed Epoetin Alfa 4000 units subcutaneously three times weekly. Provided detailed instructions for self-administration and storage. Emphasized monitoring for side effects, particularly hypertension.

Reviewed target hemoglobin range for CKD patients (10-11 g/dL). Advised to report any new headaches or vision changes. Next labs for hemoglobin and iron studies in 4 weeks. Continue all other medications. Discussed progression towards needing renal replacement therapy in the future, as GFR is critically low.

chief_complaint.value: Discussion of renal replacement therapy, fistula planning

notes.value:
Patient feeling slightly better with ESA, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL. Creatinine 2.6 mg/dL, eGFR 23 mL/min/1.73m2. Phosphorus 4.8 mg/dL, PTH 118 pg/mL. Discussed in detail the options for renal replacement therapy: hemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis, or transplant. Patient expressed preference for hemodialysis.

Given declining GFR and likelihood of needing dialysis within the next 6-12 months, initiation of dialysis access planning is crucial. Explained AV fistula creation procedure and maturation time. Referred to Vascular Surgery for consultation and evaluation for AV fistula placement. Continue current medical management. Follow-up in 6 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Vascular surgery consult - AV fistula creation

notes.value:
Vascular surgery consultation for hemodialysis access. Reviewed patient's medical history, CKD progression, and preference for AV fistula. Physical examination of upper extremities showed suitable veins for fistula creation, with palpable radial and ulnar pulses. Doppler ultrasound confirmed vessel patency and size.

Discussed risks and benefits of AV fistula creation, including potential for failure to mature, infection, and steal syndrome. Patient understands the procedure and the need for a 6-8 week maturation period. Consent obtained for left radiocephalic AV fistula creation. Scheduled for surgery in approximately 5 weeks. Pre-operative instructions provided. Refer to nephrology for continued medical management.

Patient presents for pre-operative clearance for AV fistula creation. Overall health stable. No new cardiovascular symptoms. Blood pressure well-controlled. Labs show Hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL, Creatinine 2.7 mg/dL, eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m2. Electrolytes stable. ECG normal. Cleared for elective surgery. Advised to hold Lisinopril morning of surgery. Continue all other medications.

Provided patient with pre-op instructions, including fasting guidelines. Reminded to contact surgeon for any pre-op concerns. Encouraged positive outlook regarding the procedure as a step towards managing CKD.

Patient underwent successful creation of a left radiocephalic arteriovenous fistula. Procedure performed under local anesthesia with sedation. Intraoperative findings confirmed good vessel quality. Anastomosis created without complication. Good thrill and bruit noted immediately post-operatively. Dressing applied. Patient tolerated procedure well.

Post-operative instructions included strict limb elevation, avoiding compression, and daily exercise of the hand. Pain managed with acetaminophen. Discharged home same day. Follow-up with vascular surgery in 2 weeks for wound check and initial fistula assessment.

outcome.value: Successful creation with immediate thrill and bruit

notes.value:
Patient presents for post-operative check-up 2 weeks after AV fistula creation. Incision site clean, dry, and intact, without signs of infection. Good thrill and bruit palpable along the fistula. Arm circumference stable. Patient reports mild discomfort but is otherwise doing well.

Advised continuation of daily hand exercises to promote fistula maturation. Reinforced protecting the fistula arm. Next follow-up in 4 weeks for further maturation assessment. Instructions given to contact clinic if any redness, swelling, or loss of thrill/bruit occurs. Continued coordination with nephrology.

chief_complaint.value: Nephrology follow-up - fistula maturation, CKD progression

notes.value:
Patient returns for nephrology follow-up. Fistula maturing well, good thrill. Creatinine 2.8 mg/dL, eGFR 21 mL/min/1.73m2 (further decline). Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL. Phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL. PTH 130 pg/mL. Labs indicate continued progression of CKD.

Discussion re-emphasized planning for dialysis initiation. Reviewed fistula care and maturation. Reassured patient about the process. Adjusted Epoetin Alfa dose slightly to maintain hemoglobin within target range. Encouraged patient to continue preparing for dialysis. Next visit in 6 weeks to assess fistula and further discuss dialysis planning.

PCP follow-up for general health and diabetes management. Patient feeling more fatigued lately, likely due to worsening CKD. Fistula maturing well. A1c 8.4% (worse). Blood pressure 140/85 mmHg.

Given the rising A1c and progressive CKD, initiated Insulin Glargine 10 units daily at bedtime to improve glycemic control and potentially slow renal decline. Counseled on insulin administration and monitoring for hypoglycemia. Discussed emotional impact of CKD progression and offered support resources. Encouraged close follow-up with nephrology. Will recheck A1c in 3 months.

Patient returns for nephrology follow-up. Creatinine 3.0 mg/dL, eGFR 19 mL/min/1.73m2. Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL. Fistula fully matured and suitable for cannulation. Patient reports increasing fatigue and occasional nausea, consistent with uremic symptoms. Discussed final preparations for hemodialysis initiation.

Provided schedule for first dialysis sessions. Emphasized dietary restrictions and importance of fluid balance. Answered all patient questions regarding dialysis. Commended patient on her resilience and active participation in her care. Continue all medications, particularly strict adherence to insulin regimen. Next visit will be at the dialysis unit.

summary.value: 52-year-old male with a history of progressive Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction (HFrEF), complicated by Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3, and new onset Atrial Fibrillation requiring multiple medication adjustments and two hospitalizations for decompensation.

condition_022.label: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction

name.value: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction

condition_024.label: Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response

name.value: Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response

chief_complaint.value: Increasing shortness of breath on exertion

notes.value:
Patient is a 52-year-old male presenting with a 3-month history of progressive shortness of breath, initially with heavy exertion, now with moderate activity like climbing stairs. Reports occasional mild pedal edema by end of day. Denies chest pain, palpitations, orthopnea, or PND. Past medical history notable for controlled hypertension. Social history: non-smoker, occasional alcohol. 

Physical exam reveals mild bilateral pedal edema. Lungs clear to auscultation. Heart regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Ordered CBC, CMP, BNP, TSH, and a transthoracic echocardiogram. Referral for cardiology consult pending echo results.

chief_complaint.value: Review of echo results, initiation of heart failure treatment

notes.value:
Patient returns to discuss imaging and lab results. Echo shows Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF) of 40%, consistent with Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction (HFrEF). Mild left ventricular hypertrophy noted. BNP was elevated at 450 pg/mL. Other labs within normal limits. 

Discussed diagnosis of HFrEF and importance of guideline-directed medical therapy. Started Lisinopril 5mg daily and Metoprolol Succinate ER 25mg daily. Counseled on low-sodium diet and fluid restriction. Scheduled follow-up in 2 weeks for medication tolerance and titration.

chief_complaint.value: Medication tolerance and titration for HFrEF

notes.value:
Patient reports tolerating Lisinopril and Metoprolol Succinate ER well, with only mild initial dizziness that has resolved. No worsening of shortness of breath or edema. Blood pressure 128/80 mmHg, heart rate 68 bpm. 

Titrated Lisinopril to 10mg daily. Ordered repeat CMP to monitor renal function and potassium. Reviewed importance of continuing lifestyle modifications. Scheduled cardiology consult next month.

Patient seen for initial cardiology consultation for HFrEF. Reviewed PCP notes, echo, and current medication regimen. Patient reports slight improvement in dyspnea but still limited. Exam reveals S3 gallop, mild JVD. 

Concur with current GDMT. Plan to continue titrating Lisinopril and Metoprolol Succinate ER to target doses. Recommend repeat echo in 3 months to assess EF. Discussed potential for additional therapies if EF remains reduced. Emphasized strict adherence to fluid/sodium restriction.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent dyspnea on exertion despite medication titration

notes.value:
Patient reports some continued dyspnea, particularly with sustained activity. Lisinopril increased to 15mg daily. Metoprolol Succinate ER increased to 50mg daily. Blood pressure 122/78 mmHg, heart rate 62 bpm. No new symptoms. 

Ordered repeat BNP and CMP. Encouraged continued adherence to diet and exercise. Discussed the slow process of improving heart function. Advised to notify immediately if significant worsening of symptoms or weight gain occurs.

Patient seen for cardiology follow-up. Repeat echocardiogram performed today. LVEF now estimated at 35%, a slight decline despite medication titrations. Symptoms remain stable but persistent. 

Due to persistent low EF, initiated Spironolactone 25mg daily. Counseled on potential for hyperkalemia and need for close lab monitoring. Titrated Lisinopril to target dose of 20mg daily. Continue Metoprolol Succinate ER 50mg daily. Repeat CMP in 1 week to monitor for hyperkalemia.

Patient reports tolerating Spironolactone well. No signs of hyperkalemia. Repeat CMP shows Potassium 4.8 mEq/L (normal) and Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL (mildly increased from baseline, eGFR 65 mL/min). 

Continue all current medications. Reinforce strict low-sodium diet. Discussed the importance of monitoring renal function with these medications. Follow up with cardiology in 2 months. Advised to call if any signs of fluid overload (e.g., significant weight gain, worsening dyspnea) occur.

chief_complaint.value: Acute worsening dyspnea, increased pedal edema

notes.value:
Patient presented to ED with rapidly worsening shortness of breath over 2 days, now with orthopnea and marked increase in bilateral pedal edema (3+ pitting). Reports a 5lb weight gain since last week. BP 150/90 mmHg, HR 98 bpm, RR 24, O2 Sat 90% on room air. 

Physical exam reveals crackles at lung bases, elevated JVP, and significant peripheral edema. ECG showed sinus tachycardia. Chest X-ray revealed cardiomegaly and pulmonary edema. Labs: BNP 1200 pg/mL, Creatinine 1.5 mg/dL. Admitted to hospital for acute decompensated heart failure.

chief_complaint.value: Hospital admission for acute decompensated heart failure

notes.value:
Patient admitted with acute decompensated HFrEF. Initiated IV Furosemide with good diuresis. Strict I/Os monitored. Creatinine initially 1.5 mg/dL, declined to 1.3 mg/dL after diuresis. Based on sustained elevated creatinine (eGFR ~45-50 mL/min), diagnosed with Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3. 

Cardiology team consulted nephrology for co-management given kidney involvement. All GDMT for HF were continued, careful monitoring of renal function and electrolytes. Patient's symptoms gradually improved over several days.

Patient is stable for discharge. Significant fluid offload achieved. Dyspnea resolved, no pedal edema. Creatinine stable at 1.3 mg/dL. Discharged on revised medication list including Furosemide 40mg twice daily, along with continued Lisinopril 20mg, Metoprolol Succinate ER 50mg, and Spironolactone 25mg. 

Extensive patient education provided regarding medication adherence, daily weights, strict fluid/sodium restriction, and follow-up appointments with PCP, cardiology, and nephrology. Given written instructions and red flag symptoms.

Patient seen for post-hospitalization follow-up. Reports feeling much better, no recurrent dyspnea or edema. Adhering to medication regimen and dietary restrictions. Weight stable. 

Reviewed all discharge medications. Labs show Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, Potassium 4.5 mEq/L, Sodium 138 mEq/L. Discussed the need for continued vigilance with heart failure management and CKD. Confirmed follow-up with cardiology and nephrology. Continue current plan.

Patient stable post-HF exacerbation. Current medications are well-tolerated. Reviewed current GDMT. Decided to switch Metoprolol Succinate ER to Carvedilol 25mg twice daily, as Carvedilol has stronger evidence in HFrEF. 

Patient was educated on the new medication and titration schedule. Plan to monitor blood pressure and heart rate closely. Will recheck labs in 2 weeks. Discussed potential for SGLT2 inhibitor in the future if renal function permits.

chief_complaint.value: Nephrology consult for CKD Stage 3 management

notes.value:
Patient seen for initial nephrology consult for CKD Stage 3, eGFR averaging 48 mL/min. Reviewed complete medical history and current medication list, particularly ACE inhibitor and MRA. 

Discussed strategies for renal protection including strict BP control, avoiding NSAIDs, and continued lifestyle modifications. Emphasized close monitoring of renal function and electrolytes with HF medications. No immediate changes to current GDMT recommended. Follow-up in 3 months with repeat labs.

chief_complaint.value: Sudden onset palpitations, dizziness, and dyspnea

notes.value:
Patient presented to ED with acute onset of rapid, irregular heartbeats, associated with dizziness and worsening dyspnea, starting approximately 4 hours ago. BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 160 bpm, O2 Sat 92% on room air. 

ECG revealed Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response (AFib with RVR). Chest X-ray showed no acute pulmonary edema. Labs: BNP elevated at 850 pg/mL. Admitted to hospital for rate control and initiation of anticoagulation. Cardizem drip initiated.

chief_complaint.value: Hospital admission for new onset Atrial Fibrillation

notes.value:
Patient admitted for new onset AFib with RVR. Successfully rate-controlled with Diltiazem. Reviewed patient's CHA2DS2-VASc score (e.g., age, HF, HTN) indicating high risk for stroke. Initiated Apixaban 5mg twice daily for anticoagulation. 

Repeat echocardiogram performed shows LVEF now 25%, further decline. Discussed need for continued aggressive GDMT for HFrEF. Patient educated on new medications, particularly anticoagulation and the risks/benefits. Plan for discharge in 2 days.

Patient is stable for discharge. AFib rate controlled on oral Diltiazem ER 180mg daily. No further palpitations or dizziness. Anticoagulation with Apixaban is well-established. Discharged on his full regimen: Lisinopril 20mg, Furosemide 40mg BID, Spironolactone 25mg, Carvedilol 25mg BID, Apixaban 5mg BID, Diltiazem ER 180mg. 

Reinforced strict adherence to all medications, particularly anticoagulation. Provided detailed discharge instructions, including signs of bleeding. Scheduled follow-up with PCP and cardiology in 2 weeks, and nephrology in 1 month.

Patient seen for follow-up after recent hospitalization for new onset AFib and further decline in EF. Reports feeling well, no palpitations, dyspnea is stable. Tolerating all new medications without significant side effects. 

Heart rate is well-controlled at ~70-80 bpm. Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg. Reinforce importance of lifelong anticoagulation. Discussed potential for future AFib ablation given young age and new onset. Will continue to monitor EF and symptoms. Next cardiology follow-up in 3 months.

value.value: Atrial Fibrillation with controlled ventricular response

notes.value:
Patient seen for routine follow-up with PCP. Reports stable symptoms, no fluid overload, good energy levels. Adhering to all 6 medications. BP 120/75 mmHg, HR 72 bpm. 

Reviewed all medications, addressing any potential drug-drug interactions or side effects. Emphasized adherence and continued communication between specialists. Labs today: Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (eGFR 46 mL/min), Potassium 4.6 mEq/L, Sodium 137 mEq/L. BNP 600 pg/mL (improved from crisis). Continue multidisciplinary approach to care.

summary.value: 38-year-old female presenting with acute vision loss and paresthesias, diagnosed with Relapsing-Remitting Multiple Sclerosis after MRI and lumbar puncture, managed with disease-modifying therapies and later addressed for depression.

chief_complaint.value: Acute vision loss in left eye

notes.value:
Patient presents with sudden onset of blurred vision and pain with eye movement in her left eye for the past 2 days. Reports vision is significantly dimmed, like looking through a fog. No history of similar episodes or known neurological conditions. Denies fever, headache, or recent trauma. Otherwise healthy, no other focal neurological symptoms. 

Visual acuity tested: Right eye 20/20, Left eye 20/80. Funduscopic exam shows mild optic disc edema in the left eye. Pupils equal and reactive to light, but relative afferent pupillary defect noted in the left eye. Urgent referral to ophthalmology made for suspected optic neuritis.

Patient seen in ophthalmology for acute left eye vision loss. Confirmed findings of optic neuritis: decreased visual acuity (20/80 left eye), dyschromatopsia, and left relative afferent pupillary defect. Full neurological exam performed, revealing no other cranial nerve palsies or motor deficits at this time. 

Given the presentation, MS-related optic neuritis is a strong consideration. Discussed the need for urgent neurological evaluation, brain MRI, and potential lumbar puncture. Initiated high-dose IV methylprednisolone for 3 days to hasten visual recovery. Referred to neurology for further workup.

New patient consultation for suspected Multiple Sclerosis, referred after diagnosis of optic neuritis. Patient reports mild improvement in left eye vision after IV steroids but still significantly impaired. Denies any previous episodes of neurological symptoms, though upon closer questioning, recalls transient numbness in her left arm 2 years ago that resolved spontaneously.

Neurological exam shows residual left relative afferent pupillary defect. No other focal motor or sensory deficits. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ symmetrical. Plantar responses flexor. Ordered urgent MRI of the brain and cervical/thoracic spine with and without contrast to evaluate for demyelinating lesions. Also ordered extensive labs, including inflammatory markers, Vitamin B12, and ANA, to rule out mimics. Started a 3-day course of high-dose IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy again, as vision had not fully recovered.

chief_complaint.value: MRI review and further workup for MS

notes.value:
Patient returns for review of MRI findings. Brain MRI reveals multiple T2 hyperintense lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial regions, some of which demonstrate contrast enhancement, indicative of active demyelination. Spinal cord MRI shows a non-enhancing T2 lesion in the cervical spine. These findings are highly suggestive of Multiple Sclerosis.

Labs (CBC, CMP, B12, ANA, ESR, CRP) were all within normal limits, ruling out other inflammatory or autoimmune conditions. Left eye vision has significantly improved after steroid course, now 20/30. Discussed plans for a lumbar puncture to assess cerebrospinal fluid for oligoclonal bands. Patient counseled on the high likelihood of MS diagnosis.

outcome.value: Multiple T2 hyperintense demyelinating lesions, some enhancing

procedure_012.label: MRI Cervical/Thoracic Spine with & without contrast

name.value: MRI Cervical/Thoracic Spine with & without contrast

outcome.value: One non-enhancing T2 lesion in cervical spine

chief_complaint.value: Lumbar puncture results, MS diagnosis and DMT discussion

notes.value:
Patient returns for lumbar puncture results and discussion of definitive diagnosis. CSF analysis reveals 5 oligoclonal bands present in CSF but not in serum, confirming intrathecal IgG synthesis. Combined with clinical presentation (optic neuritis) and MRI findings (dissemination in space and time), a definitive diagnosis of Relapsing-Remitting Multiple Sclerosis (RRMS) is established.

Thorough discussion regarding disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) for RRMS. Patient has several questions regarding side effects and administration. Decided to start with Interferon beta-1a (Avonex) as a first-line injectable DMT. Patient to undergo training for self-injection and will follow up in 2 weeks to initiate treatment.

value.value: Positive (5 bands in CSF, none in serum)

outcome.value: CSF positive for oligoclonal bands

notes.value:
Patient returns for initiation of Interferon beta-1a. Self-injection training provided by nurse educator. Patient demonstrated competence with injection technique. Discussed common side effects including flu-like symptoms, injection site reactions, and potential liver enzyme elevation. 

Prescription for Interferon beta-1a (Avonex) 44mcg intramuscularly once weekly provided. Baseline labs (CBC, LFTs) ordered to monitor for adverse effects. Patient instructed to report any severe side effects or new neurological symptoms promptly. Follow-up in 3 months.

Patient seen for routine check-up and discussion of ongoing fatigue, which she attributes partially to the interferon. Reports flu-like symptoms after injections have lessened but still present. Continues Vitamin D screening due to increased risk in MS patients. 

Labs reveal Vitamin D level of 22 ng/mL (deficient). Other routine labs within normal limits. Started Vitamin D3 5000 IU daily supplementation. Encouraged to maintain a healthy lifestyle and discuss fatigue management strategies with her neurologist. Follow up in 3 months or as needed.

Patient returns for 3-month follow-up after initiating Interferon beta-1a. Reports generally tolerating the medication; flu-like symptoms are now milder and less frequent. No new neurological symptoms or relapses reported. Continues to experience mild fatigue but states it is manageable.

Neurological exam stable. Labs (CBC, LFTs) show no significant abnormalities related to interferon. Patient's Vitamin D levels were noted as low at last PCP visit, and supplementation was started. Reinforced importance of adherence to DMT and lifestyle modifications. Next routine follow-up in 6 months, or sooner if new symptoms develop.

chief_complaint.value: New onset numbness and tingling in legs

notes.value:
Patient calls urgently reporting new onset of progressive numbness and tingling sensations in both legs, extending from her feet to her knees, over the past 3 days. Also notes difficulty with balance. Denies fever, weakness, or bladder dysfunction. This is a clear relapse of MS symptoms.

On neurological exam, decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in bilateral lower extremities in a stocking-glove distribution. Romberg sign positive, gait mildly ataxic. Urgent MRI of the brain and spine with contrast ordered to assess for new lesions. Initiated 3-day course of high-dose IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy for the acute relapse.

chief_complaint.value: MRI results after relapse, DMT switch

notes.value:
Patient returns after completing steroid course for relapse. Numbness and tingling have partially improved, but residual symptoms persist. MRI brain with contrast shows 3 new gadolinium-enhancing lesions, indicating active disease despite Interferon beta-1a. This represents a treatment failure.

Discussed switching to a more potent disease-modifying therapy. After reviewing options, patient opted for Fingolimod (Gilenya), an oral DMT, due to efficacy and convenience. Counseled on baseline requirements (ECG, eye exam, varicella zoster serology) and first-dose monitoring. Interferon beta-1a discontinued. Started Fingolimod after completing required screening.

outcome.value: 3 new gadolinium-enhancing lesions

chief_complaint.value: Fingolimod baseline workup and first-dose observation

notes.value:
Patient underwent baseline workup for Fingolimod initiation: ECG showed normal sinus rhythm, VZV IgG serology was positive (immune to chickenpox). Baseline ophthalmology exam for macular edema screening was normal. First dose of Fingolimod 0.5mg administered under observation in the clinic.

Patient tolerated the first dose well, with no bradycardia or other adverse events during the 6-hour monitoring period. Instructed on daily oral administration and potential side effects. Regular monitoring of CBC and LFTs will be performed. Scheduled next follow-up in 3 months.

outcome.value: Normal sinus rhythm
Tolerated well, no acute adverse events

notes.value:
Patient presents reporting persistent low mood, loss of interest in activities she used to enjoy (anhedonia), and difficulty sleeping for the past 4-6 weeks. States she feels overwhelmed by her MS diagnosis and recent relapse. Denies suicidal ideation but expresses feelings of hopelessness. 

Screening questionnaire (PHQ-9) score is 18, indicative of moderate to severe depression. Discussed initiating antidepressant therapy and referral to psychiatry for comprehensive mental health assessment. Provided resources for support groups for chronic illness. Scheduled psychiatry consult.

New patient psychiatry consultation for evaluation of depressive symptoms. Patient openly discusses feelings of sadness, anhedonia, fatigue, and difficulty concentrating following her MS diagnosis and recent relapse. Symptoms have significantly impacted her quality of life and ability to cope with her chronic condition. 

Mental status exam consistent with moderate depressive episode. Diagnosis of Moderate Single Episode Depression (F32.1) made. Initiated Sertraline 50mg daily. Patient educated on medication expectations and potential side effects. Scheduled follow-up in 4 weeks to assess response and titrate dosage if necessary. Also discussed potential for psychotherapy.

chief_complaint.value: 3-month neurology follow-up on Fingolimod, new neuropathic pain

notes.value:
Patient returns for 3-month follow-up after switching to Fingolimod. Reports no new relapses or worsening of previous symptoms. Vision remains stable. However, new complaint of persistent burning and tingling sensations in her feet, consistent with neuropathic pain. 

Neurological exam shows improved sensation in lower extremities compared to relapse, but still some residual sensory deficits. Pain rated 4/10. Initiated Gabapentin 300mg twice daily for neuropathic pain. Discussed referral to physical therapy for gait and balance assessment, given her history of leg weakness and balance issues during the last relapse. Continue Fingolimod. Next follow-up in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: Physical therapy consult for gait and balance

notes.value:
Patient seen for initial physical therapy evaluation. Reports mild residual numbness in feet and occasional balance issues, especially on uneven surfaces. Goal is to improve gait stability and reduce fall risk. 

Assessment reveals mild impairment in tandem gait and single-leg stance. Strength 4+/5 in bilateral lower extremities. No significant spasticity. Initiated a personalized exercise program focusing on core strengthening, balance exercises (e.g., tai chi, wobble board), and gait training with visual cues. Patient to perform exercises at home daily. Scheduled follow-up sessions weekly for 6 weeks.

outcome.value: Mildly unsteady, decreased proprioception
Impaired on uneven surfaces, positive Romberg

notes.value:
Patient returns for follow-up on Sertraline. Reports significant improvement in mood, energy levels, and overall interest in life. States she feels more able to cope with her chronic illness. Sleep has improved. Denies side effects from medication. PHQ-9 score is now 6, indicating mild depression. 

Continue Sertraline 50mg daily. Encouraged to continue physical therapy and self-care strategies. Discussed the option of increasing the dose if symptoms worsen, but for now, stable on current regimen. Next follow-up in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: Annual neurology check-up, MS disease stability

notes.value:
Annual neurology check-up. Patient reports stable condition on Fingolimod and Gabapentin. No new relapses or worsening of existing symptoms. Mood is much improved with Sertraline. Continues with physical therapy exercises at home. 

Neurological exam is stable with no new deficits. Labs (CBC, LFTs) are within normal limits. Vitamin D levels are now within normal range at 35 ng/mL. Discussed upcoming annual brain MRI for disease surveillance. Reinforced medication adherence and healthy lifestyle. Next routine follow-up in 6 months.

summary.value: 68-year-old male with a history of COPD, incidentally found to have Stage IIIA Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (adenocarcinoma) during evaluation for a COPD exacerbation. Underwent concurrent chemoradiation, complicated by a pulmonary embolism requiring anticoagulation. Currently on maintenance therapy and anticoagulation.

chief_complaint.value: Worsening shortness of breath, increased cough with sputum

notes.value:
Patient presents with a 5-day history of increased dyspnea, particularly with exertion, and a productive cough with yellow sputum, consistent with a COPD exacerbation. Reports using his rescue inhaler more frequently without significant relief. Vital signs: T 99.2 F, HR 98, RR 24, SpO2 90% on room air. Lungs with diffuse wheezes and prolonged expiration. 

Initiated oral prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days. Prescribed a 5-day course of Azithromycin. Ordered chest X-ray to rule out pneumonia. Advised to continue maintenance inhalers and follow up in 3 days for X-ray review and symptom reassessment.

chief_complaint.value: COPD exacerbation follow-up, chest X-ray review

notes.value:
Patient returns for follow-up. Symptoms are marginally improved but still significant dyspnea. Chest X-ray shows a new, ill-defined opacity in the right upper lobe, not typical for an acute infection. No frank pneumonia. Given the patient's smoking history and age, further imaging is warranted.

Discontinued prednisone. Ordered a contrast-enhanced Chest CT to further characterize the right upper lobe opacity. Referral to pulmonology for evaluation of lung mass. Patient advised to complete antibiotic course and continue COPD management. Will contact with CT results and pulmonology appointment details.

outcome.value: New ill-defined opacity in right upper lobe.

chief_complaint.value: Chest CT results review, ongoing dyspnea

notes.value:
Patient reviewed today. Chest CT results show a 4.5 cm spiculated mass in the right upper lobe with associated hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Findings are highly suspicious for malignancy. 

Patient counseled on the findings and the high suspicion for lung cancer. Pulmonology appointment with Dr. James Miller has been scheduled for November 5th. Emphasized the importance of this consultation for further diagnostic workup. Continued supportive care for COPD.

outcome.value: 4.5 cm spiculated mass in RUL, hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

notes.value:
New patient consult for evaluation of right upper lobe lung mass. Reviewed Chest CT images. Discussed patient's smoking history and COPD. Physical exam unremarkable apart from diminished breath sounds at bases. 

Recommended diagnostic bronchoscopy with endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) guided biopsy of the mass and mediastinal lymph nodes. Patient consented to the procedure. Discussed risks and benefits. Procedure scheduled for next week. Patient advised on pre-procedure fasting and medication adjustments.

Patient underwent bronchoscopy with EBUS-guided transbronchial needle aspiration (TBNA) of the right upper lobe mass and mediastinal lymph nodes (stations 4R and 7). Procedure tolerated well without complications. Tissue samples sent for histopathological analysis, immunohistochemistry, and molecular testing. 

Patient discharged home after recovery, stable. Advised to report any fever, hemoptysis, or increasing shortness of breath. Follow-up scheduled with pulmonology for results review.

outcome.value: Samples obtained from RUL mass and mediastinal lymph nodes without complication.

notes.value:
Patient returned for review of biopsy results. Pathology confirms Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, adenocarcinoma subtype, with positive lymph node involvement in station 4R. Molecular testing for EGFR, ALK, ROS1 pending. 

Patient counseled on the diagnosis of lung cancer. Explained the importance of further staging to guide treatment. Referral placed to oncology for comprehensive staging and treatment planning. Patient provided with resources for support. Oncologist consult scheduled.

chief_complaint.value: Oncology consult for newly diagnosed NSCLC

notes.value:
New patient oncology consult for adenocarcinoma of the lung. Reviewed outside records and biopsy results confirming NSCLC with regional lymph node involvement. Patient's performance status is good (ECOG 1). 

Ordered comprehensive staging workup: PET/CT scan to assess for distant metastases and brain MRI to rule out brain metastases. Discussed preliminary treatment options based on potential staging. Will review all imaging and molecular results at next visit to finalize a treatment plan. Patient given contact information for questions.

Patient seen for review of staging scans. PET/CT shows hypermetabolic activity only in the right upper lobe mass and associated hilar/mediastinal lymph nodes, no evidence of distant metastases. Brain MRI is negative for metastatic disease. Molecular testing showed no actionable mutations (EGFR/ALK/ROS1 negative).

Diagnosis confirmed as Stage IIIA Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (T2b N2 M0). Discussed multidisciplinary treatment approach including concurrent chemoradiation. Proposed regimen: Carboplatin + Pemetrexed every 3 weeks for 4 cycles, concurrently with thoracic radiation. Referral to Radiation Oncology for simulation and planning. Patient agreeable to treatment plan. Provided extensive education on treatment course and potential side effects.

outcome.value: Hypermetabolic RUL mass and regional lymph nodes, no distant mets.
No evidence of brain metastases.

notes.value:
New patient radiation oncology consult. Reviewed CT and PET scans. Discussed concurrent chemoradiation plan for Stage IIIA NSCLC. Patient underwent radiation simulation today for treatment planning. Target volumes (GTV, CTV, PTV) delineated for IMRT. 

Discussed potential side effects, including esophagitis, pneumonitis, and fatigue. Consent obtained. Treatment expected to start next week, coordinated with medical oncology for chemotherapy cycles.

outcome.value: Completed, planning for IMRT.

notes.value:
Patient initiated concurrent chemoradiation today. Received first cycle of Carboplatin (AUC 6) and Pemetrexed (500 mg/m²) intravenously. Pre-medications given including ondansetron. Tolerated infusion well with no immediate adverse reactions. 

Started daily radiation treatments today. Patient counseled again on common side effects such as nausea, fatigue, myelosuppression, and skin irritation. Labs (CBC, CMP) ordered prior to treatment and will be monitored weekly. Scheduled for next chemotherapy cycle in 3 weeks. Provided anti-emetics for home use.

chief_complaint.value: Chemotherapy Cycle 2, new dyspnea and leg swelling

notes.value:
Patient presented for Cycle 2 of Carboplatin/Pemetrexed. Reports increasing dyspnea over the past 3 days, now persistent at rest, and new onset right leg swelling. Denies chest pain, cough, or fever. Radiation is ongoing.

Physical exam reveals mild tachypnea and unilateral (right) lower extremity edema, pitting to +2. Lungs with decreased breath sounds on the right. Given symptoms and hypercoagulable state of malignancy, high suspicion for pulmonary embolism or DVT. Chemotherapy held. Urgent labs ordered including D-dimer. Immediate referral to Emergency Department for further evaluation.

chief_complaint.value: Acute dyspnea, right leg swelling, suspected PE

notes.value:
Patient presented to ED from oncology clinic with acute dyspnea and right leg swelling. Vitals: HR 110, RR 26, SpO2 88% on room air. D-dimer elevated at 1250 ng/mL FEU. CT Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA) performed, confirming multiple bilateral pulmonary emboli, largest in the right main pulmonary artery. Right lower extremity ultrasound negative for DVT. 

Diagnosis: Acute Pulmonary Embolism. Initiated therapeutic anticoagulation with Apixaban 10mg BID for 7 days, then 5mg BID. Patient admitted for observation and cardiology consult. Oncology team notified.

outcome.value: Multiple bilateral pulmonary emboli confirmed.
Negative for DVT.

notes.value:
Cardiology consult for newly diagnosed pulmonary embolism in a patient with active lung cancer. Reviewed CTPA and clinical status. Patient is hemodynamically stable. No evidence of right heart strain. 

Confirmed plan for long-term anticoagulation with Apixaban for at least 6 months, likely longer given active malignancy. Discussed risks of bleeding versus recurrence. Recommended close monitoring of CBC and renal function. Oncology team consulted for chemotherapy continuation strategy. Patient educated on signs/symptoms of bleeding and PE recurrence.

Patient presented for Cycle 3 of chemoradiation. PE diagnosed and successfully anticoagulated with Apixaban. Patient reports improved dyspnea and no further leg swelling. Labs today show improving counts: WBC 4.1, Hgb 11.5, Plt 130. Creatinine stable.

Reviewed with cardiology and radiation oncology. Agreed to proceed with Cycle 3 of Carboplatin/Pemetrexed, carefully monitoring for bleeding complications due to concurrent anticoagulation.
