Radiation therapy continuing without interruption. Patient tolerating Apixaban well. Emphasized adherence to both cancer treatment and anticoagulation.

Patient presented for Cycle 4 (final cycle) of Carboplatin/Pemetrexed. Overall feeling well. No new complaints. Radiation is nearing completion. Continues on Apixaban without any bleeding complications. 

Labs today within acceptable range for chemotherapy. Administered final cycle of systemic chemotherapy. Discussed plan for post-treatment evaluation scans approximately 4-6 weeks after completion of both chemotherapy and radiation. Patient remains engaged and motivated.

Patient completed 30 fractions of definitive radiation therapy for Stage IIIA NSCLC today. Tolerated treatment well with expected side effects of fatigue and mild esophagitis, which are resolving. Skin reaction minimal. 

Discussed acute and late radiation side effects. Patient instructed on supportive care measures. Follow-up with medical oncology for post-treatment surveillance scans. Provided contact for any questions or new symptoms.

Patient seen for post-treatment surveillance planning. Approximately 5 weeks since completion of chemoradiation. Reports improved energy levels, resolution of esophagitis, and stable respiratory status. Continues on Apixaban. 

Ordered repeat PET/CT scan to assess treatment response and rule out recurrence or progression. Also ordered a baseline CT chest without contrast for future surveillance. Labs (CBC, CMP) checked prior to imaging. Scheduled for follow-up in 1 week to review scan results.

chief_complaint.value: Review of post-treatment PET/CT and surveillance plan

notes.value:
Patient returns for review of post-treatment PET/CT scan. Scan shows significant metabolic and size reduction of the right upper lobe mass and mediastinal lymph nodes, consistent with good partial response to therapy. No new hypermetabolic foci suggesting recurrence or metastases. 

Discussed findings. Plan for ongoing surveillance with CT chest without contrast every 3 months for the first year. Continue Apixaban indefinitely for cancer-associated thrombosis prophylaxis. Patient expressed relief and understanding of the surveillance plan. Next oncology follow-up in 3 months.

outcome.value: Significant partial response of RUL mass and lymph nodes, no new disease.

summary.value: 44-year-old female with longstanding Crohn's Disease with progressive stricturing behavior, requiring small bowel resection. Patient also suffers from iron deficiency anemia and osteoporosis secondary to chronic corticosteroid use.

medication_058.label: Calcium Carbonate/Vitamin D3 1200mg Calcium / 800 IU Vit D3

chief_complaint.value: Worsening abdominal pain and diarrhea for 2 months

notes.value: Patient presents with escalating lower abdominal pain, cramping, and increased frequency of watery stools (4-5 times daily) for the past two months. Reports occasional blood in stool. Known history of Crohn's Disease for 10 years, currently on Mesalamine 1.2g TID. Has lost 5 lbs unintentionally. Denies fever, night sweats, or joint pain. Exam reveals mild right lower quadrant tenderness. Labs ordered by PCP include CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP. Given flare-up, referral to Gastroenterology initiated.

New patient consultation for Crohn's flare. Patient symptoms consistent with moderate-to-severe disease activity. Reviewed PCP's labs: WBC 8.5, Hgb 10.8 (low), Platelets 350, ESR 45 (high), CRP 1.8 (high). Ordered additional labs including Fecal Calprotectin, Iron Panel, and Vitamin D levels. Discussed current Mesalamine regimen, noting it's typically for mild-to-moderate disease. Plan to review all labs and consider escalation of therapy. Continue Mesalamine for now.

Patient returns for lab review. Fecal Calprotectin 850 mcg/g (elevated), Iron Saturation 12% (low), Ferritin 18 ng/mL (low), Vitamin D 25 ng/mL (insufficient). Diagnosis of Iron Deficiency Anemia confirmed. Given persistent symptoms and high inflammatory markers despite Mesalamine, therapy escalated. Started Azathioprine 50mg daily. Also prescribed Prednisone 40mg daily, with a planned 4-week taper, to induce remission. Patient educated on potential side effects and need for regular lab monitoring with Azathioprine. Advised on dietary iron intake and Vitamin D supplementation (OTC).

chief_complaint.value: PCP check-in, starting new Crohn's medications

notes.value: Patient presents for routine check-in after starting Azathioprine and Prednisone. Reports feeling somewhat better, abdominal pain has decreased, and diarrhea frequency is down to 2-3 times daily. Tolerating new medications well, no immediate side effects. Discussed starting Iron Sulfate for anemia given persistently low iron levels despite dietary efforts. Reinforced adherence to medication schedule and importance of follow-up with GI. Ordered baseline liver function tests for Azathioprine monitoring.

chief_complaint.value: Recurrence of symptoms after prednisone taper

notes.value: Patient returns, having completed prednisone taper 5 days ago. Reports rapid recurrence of abdominal pain and cramping, similar to initial flare. Also experienced an episode of postprandial vomiting, raising concern for partial obstruction. On exam, right lower quadrant tenderness more pronounced, with guarding. Given the swift relapse and obstructive symptoms, concern for stricturing disease has increased significantly. Ordered MR Enterography to evaluate for active inflammation and strictures in the small bowel.

chief_complaint.value: MR Enterography review; terminal ileal stricture identified

notes.value: Reviewed MR Enterography results. Imaging demonstrates active inflammation with significant wall thickening and a high-grade stricture in the terminal ileum, approximately 5 cm in length. Proximal bowel dilatation noted, consistent with partial obstruction. Discussed findings with patient, explaining the severity and limited efficacy of current therapies. Options presented: escalation to biologic therapy (Infliximab) or surgical resection. Patient opts to try biologic therapy first, given desire to avoid surgery if possible. Plan for Infliximab initiation.

outcome.value: Terminal ileal stricture with active inflammation and proximal dilatation

notes.value: Patient here for the first dose of Infliximab. Pre-infusion labs reviewed, no contraindications. Patient educated on potential infusion reactions and long-term side effects. Infusion tolerated well with no immediate adverse events. Discussed discontinuation of Mesalamine, as Infliximab and Azathioprine represent a more potent combination therapy. Scheduled next infusion in 2 weeks. Continue Azathioprine and Iron Sulfate.

Patient reports some improvement in symptoms since starting Infliximab – fewer episodes of severe pain, and less frequent diarrhea. Still experiencing intermittent mild abdominal discomfort and bloating, especially after meals. Discussed impact of chronic inflammation and corticosteroid use on bone density. Recommended a DEXA scan to assess for osteoporosis, given history of multiple prednisone courses. Continued monitoring of anemia.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent symptoms despite Infliximab; surgical referral

notes.value: Patient has completed 3 loading doses of Infliximab. While there's been some improvement, patient continues to experience frequent episodes of partial obstruction, with associated pain and bloating. Repeat inflammatory markers show partial response (CRP 0.9, ESR 28), but not full remission. Given the persistent high-grade terminal ileal stricture and refractory symptoms despite optimal medical therapy, surgical intervention is now strongly recommended. Referral placed to Dr. Emily White, General Surgery, for evaluation for ileocolic resection.

Patient consulted for surgical evaluation of terminal ileal Crohn's stricture. Reviewed medical history, imaging, and extensive medication trials. Risks and benefits of ileocolic resection thoroughly discussed. Patient understands need for surgery. Pre-operative workup initiated, including updated labs, EKG, and chest X-ray. Will hold Azathioprine and Infliximab 2 weeks prior to surgery. Scheduled for surgery in mid-August.

Patient admitted for elective ileocolic resection for Crohn's stricture. Pre-operative labs within acceptable limits. Surgical procedure performed successfully without immediate complications. Resected segment revealed inflamed, strictured terminal ileum. Post-operative course was complicated by a prolonged ileus, managed conservatively with NPO status and IV fluids. Patient slowly advanced diet over several days. Discharged on post-op day 7.

outcome.value: Successful removal of strictured terminal ileum; complicated by prolonged ileus

notes.value: Follow-up visit 3 weeks post-ileocolic resection. Patient reports gradual improvement in abdominal pain and obstructive symptoms. Incision healing well. Started restarting Infliximab infusions, with first post-op dose administered yesterday. Pathology report confirms Crohn's disease involvement in the resected specimen. Discussed importance of continued Infliximab for maintenance and to prevent recurrence. Continue Azathioprine and Iron Sulfate. Plan for routine follow-up.

Reviewed DEXA scan ordered at previous visit. Results show T-score of -2.8 at the lumbar spine, consistent with osteoporosis. Discussed the implications, particularly given patient's history of chronic corticosteroid use for Crohn's flares. Started Calcium Carbonate/Vitamin D3 1200mg/800 IU daily and Alendronate 70mg weekly. Provided counseling on fall prevention, weight-bearing exercise, and dietary calcium sources. Referred to endocrinology for further management and consideration of other bone health factors.

outcome.value: T-score -2.8 (osteoporosis) at lumbar spine, -2.0 (osteopenia) at femoral neck

notes.value: New patient consultation for osteoporosis. Reviewed DEXA scan, medication history, and lifestyle factors. Patient is compliant with Calcium/Vitamin D and Alendronate. Discussed secondary causes of osteoporosis, confirming corticosteroid-induced bone loss as the primary etiology. Ordered baseline PTH, 24-hour urine calcium, and repeat Vitamin D to ensure comprehensive metabolic workup. Will monitor bone density with repeat DEXA in 1-2 years. Continue current medications.

Patient is 4 months post-resection and stable on Infliximab and Azathioprine. Reports feeling significantly better overall. No abdominal pain, no diarrhea. Energy levels improved. Tolerating all medications well. Inflammatory markers are within normal limits (CRP 0.3, ESR 15). CBC shows Hgb 13.0, indicating good control of anemia. Discussed continuing current regimen. Plan for routine Infliximab infusions every 8 weeks and quarterly GI follow-ups.

chief_complaint.value: Annual physical and chronic disease management

notes.value: Patient presents for annual physical. Overall health has significantly improved since Crohn's resection and initiation of Infliximab. Continues to manage osteoporosis with Alendronate and supplementation. Discussed importance of colon cancer screening given long-standing Crohn's. Reviewed medication adherence and screened for any new symptoms. Patient is feeling well and active. Coordinated care with GI and Endocrinology specialists.

Quarterly follow-up for Crohn's Disease. Patient remains in clinical remission. No abdominal pain, diarrhea, or other inflammatory symptoms. Infliximab infusions are ongoing without issues. Labs show continued normalization of inflammatory markers and hemoglobin. Discussed long-term prognosis and importance of ongoing surveillance. Patient is encouraged by the stable disease course. Next follow-up scheduled for 3 months.

summary.value: 75-year-old male with progressive Parkinson's Disease over 3 years, complicated by orthostatic hypotension and major depressive disorder, managed with a combination of dopaminergic, hypotensive, and antidepressant medications.

chief_complaint.value: Right hand tremor and general slowness for 3-4 months

notes.value:
Patient, a 75-year-old male, presents with a chief complaint of a subtle tremor in his right hand, primarily noticeable at rest, and a general feeling of being slowed down in his movements over the past 3-4 months. He reports difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning shirts and a reduced arm swing when walking. Denies pain, dizziness, or weakness. Family history significant for hypertension in father and Alzheimer's in mother. No current medications aside from OTC vitamins.

Physical exam reveals a mild right-sided resting tremor, bradykinesia with finger-tapping, and slightly stooped posture. Gait is slow with reduced arm swing. Cognition appears intact. Given the progressive nature of symptoms and classic presentation, a neurological consult is warranted to investigate possible Parkinson's Disease. Ordered basic labs to rule out other causes.

chief_complaint.value: Neurology consult for suspected Parkinson's Disease

notes.value:
New patient neurology consultation for a 75-year-old male presenting with 4-month history of right-sided resting tremor and progressive bradykinesia. Patient reports associated rigidity and difficulty turning in bed. No history of stroke, head injury, or neuroleptic medication use. No clear autonomic symptoms yet. Physical exam confirms classic motor signs of Parkinson's Disease: clear right-sided resting tremor (4-5 Hz), cogwheel rigidity in right upper extremity, significant bradykinesia (slowed initiation and execution of movement, decrement in amplitude with repetitive tasks). Postural instability is mild with a single pull. Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (UPDRS) motor score 28.

Diagnosis of Parkinson's Disease made clinically. Initiated Carbidopa-Levetiracetam 25/100mg, 1 tablet three times daily. Discussed potential side effects including nausea, dizziness, and dyskinesias. Reviewed importance of medication adherence. Will follow up in 3 weeks to assess response and titrate dosage.

Patient returns for follow-up 3 weeks after starting Carbidopa-Levetiracetam. Reports mild improvement in tremor and overall mobility. Still experiencing some morning stiffness and occasional difficulty initiating gait. No significant side effects reported, tolerating medication well.

Motor exam shows improved bradykinesia, tremor is less prominent. UPDRS motor score 22. Given good tolerability and partial improvement, increased Carbidopa-Levetiracetam to 1.5 tablets three times daily. Emphasized consistency with medication timing. Reviewed non-motor symptoms like constipation which patient confirmed as present; advised increased fluid and fiber intake. Scheduled next follow-up in 4 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Routine check-up, coordination of care for Parkinson's

notes.value:
Patient seen for routine check-up and to coordinate care following Parkinson's diagnosis. Reports feeling generally better since starting medication, tremor is less noticeable. No new major concerns. Blood pressure stable. No issues with swallowing or speech reported.

Reviewed medication list with patient, confirmed adherence to Carbidopa-Levetiracetam. Discussed importance of regular exercise and maintaining good nutrition. Encouraged patient to continue communicating any new symptoms to both PCP and neurologist. Plan for regular follow-ups with neurology; PCP to monitor general health and blood pressure.

Patient reports that despite Carbidopa-Levetiracetam, he experiences periods of increased stiffness and tremor before his next dose, indicating a 'wearing-off' phenomenon. He also complains of more significant constipation, with bowel movements only every 3-4 days despite dietary changes. No new falls or significant dizziness.

Motor exam confirms mild wearing-off with return of rigidity and bradykinesia approximately 30-60 minutes before scheduled dose. To address this and provide additional symptom control, initiated Rasagiline 0.5mg once daily. Also recommended Polyethylene Glycol daily for constipation. Educated patient on medication timing for Carbidopa-Levetiracetam to minimize 'off' times. Next follow-up in 6 weeks.

Patient presents with new complaint of frequent lightheadedness, especially when standing up quickly. Reports two near-falls in the past week, although no actual falls occurred. Denies chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. Hydration status appears adequate.

Orthostatic vital signs taken: Supine BP 130/80 mmHg, HR 70 bpm. Standing BP 100/60 mmHg, HR 78 bpm after 3 minutes. This represents a significant drop consistent with orthostatic hypotension. Reviewed current medications, some of which (like Rasagiline) can potentially exacerbate this. Advised patient to stand up slowly, use compression stockings, and increase fluid intake. Ordered ECG and repeat orthostatic vitals in 1 week. Consider cardiology referral if symptoms persist or worsen.

value.value: Normal Sinus Rhythm, no acute changes

notes.value:
Patient reports continued and worsening lightheadedness, now with actual falls (one minor fall at home with no injury). Orthostatic symptoms are significantly impacting daily activities and increasing anxiety about falls. Rasagiline initiated at last visit has provided some benefit for motor symptoms, but seems to be contributing to orthostatic issues.

Orthostatic vital signs today: Supine BP 125/75 mmHg, standing BP 95/55 mmHg (drop of 30 mmHg systolic). Given the severity of symptoms and documented orthostatic hypotension, urgent referral to Cardiology for further evaluation and management of this significant non-motor complication of PD. Discussed potential options like Midodrine or fludrocortisone with patient. Advised increased salt and fluid intake, and strict fall precautions.

New patient cardiology consultation for severe orthostatic hypotension in the setting of Parkinson's Disease. Reviewed PCP and Neurology notes documenting significant drops in BP upon standing, leading to near-falls and one fall. ECG from previous visit was normal.

Repeat orthostatic vital signs confirm significant drops (Supine BP 120/70, Standing BP 90/50). Excluded cardiac causes of syncope. Diagnosis of neurogenic orthostatic hypotension (NOH) related to PD. Initiated Midodrine 5mg three times daily, 30 minutes before standing, to manage NOH symptoms. Patient instructed to monitor standing blood pressure at home. Reviewed non-pharmacological interventions like increased fluid and salt intake, and careful position changes. Follow-up in 3 weeks to assess response.

Patient returns for follow-up. Reports some improvement in orthostatic symptoms with Midodrine, but still occasional dizziness. Motor symptoms have become more variable with noticeable 'wearing-off' periods, indicating need for further optimization of dopaminergic therapy.

Adjusted Carbidopa-Levetiracetam regimen to include an extended-release (ER) formulation to prolong 'on' times and reduce fluctuations. Patient will take Carbidopa-Levetiracetam 25/100mg ER twice daily, in addition to his current immediate-release (IR) doses. Midodrine dosage increased to 7.5mg three times daily, with instructions for home BP monitoring. Discussed potential for dyskinesias with increased dopaminergic load. Scheduled next follow-up in 6 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Increased apathy, low mood, sleep disturbances

notes.value:
Patient presents with new complaints of persistent low mood, loss of interest in hobbies (apathy), and difficulty falling asleep and staying asleep over the past 2 months. Reports feeling hopeless at times. Denies suicidal ideation. Daughter expresses concern for patient's overall mood and withdrawal from social activities.

Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS-15) score 11, suggestive of moderate depression. Reviewed medication list; no obvious culprits for mood changes. Given the presence of Parkinson's Disease, depression is a common non-motor complication. Discussed options including psychotherapy and pharmacotherapy. Recommend psychiatric evaluation for formal diagnosis and management. Provided referral information. Patient open to seeking mental health support.

chief_complaint.value: Psychiatry consult for depression and sleep issues

notes.value:
New patient psychiatry consultation for evaluation of depression and sleep disturbances in a patient with Parkinson's Disease. Patient reports pervasive low mood, anhedonia, fatigue, and psychomotor agitation (restlessness). Daughter confirms significant change in patient's affect and activity level. Sleep architecture is fragmented.

After comprehensive assessment, diagnosed with Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent, moderate severity. Discussed treatment options, including Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs). Started Sertraline 50mg once daily in the morning to avoid exacerbating sleep issues. Counseled on potential side effects and expected time to therapeutic effect. Advised sleep hygiene measures. Follow-up in 4 weeks to assess medication response and tolerability.

chief_complaint.value: Overall Parkinson's management, gait instability, communication difficulties

notes.value:
Patient presents for overall Parkinson's management. Reports mild improvement in mood with Sertraline. Motor symptoms are stable on current Carbidopa-Levetiracetam regimen, but continues to have some gait instability and occasional freezing. Daughter notes patient has become softer spoken and sometimes difficult to understand.

Motor exam shows stable tremor and rigidity, but still notable postural instability and mild freezing of gait. Speech is hypophonic. Given these ongoing issues, recommended comprehensive therapy evaluation. Referred to Physical Therapy for gait and balance training, Occupational Therapy for ADL strategies, and Speech-Language Pathology for dysphagia and dysarthria evaluation. Emphasized importance of proactive rehabilitation. Scheduled next follow-up in 2 months.

chief_complaint.value: Initial Physical Therapy assessment for gait and balance

notes.value:
Patient seen for initial Physical Therapy assessment following referral for gait instability and fall prevention. Patient reports fear of falling limits his outdoor activities. Timed Up and Go (TUG) test 18 seconds (high risk for falls). Balance assessment reveals impaired static and dynamic balance. Gait analysis shows reduced step length, shuffling, and decreased arm swing.

Developed a comprehensive rehabilitation plan focusing on balance exercises, gait retraining (cueing strategies), strengthening exercises, and education on fall prevention strategies for the home environment. Will work on improving postural control and reducing freezing episodes. Scheduled twice-weekly sessions for 6 weeks. Encouraged home exercise program. Will communicate progress with referring neurologist.

outcome.value: 18 seconds (high fall risk)
Impaired static and dynamic balance

chief_complaint.value: Management of motor fluctuations and non-motor symptoms

notes.value:
Patient returns for follow-up, approximately 3 months after starting Sertraline and initiating therapies. Reports moderate improvement in mood and sleep. Physical therapy is ongoing, with slight improvement in balance. Continue to manage motor fluctuations, but patient reports overall 'on' time is better with ER formulation.

Motor exam shows continued presence of tremor and bradykinesia, but severity is generally stable. UPDRS motor score 25. No new dyskinesias observed. Orthostatic BPs remain stable with Midodrine. Encouraged continued adherence to all therapies and medications. Discussed potential future deep brain stimulation (DBS) as an option if motor fluctuations become intractable, but not considered at this time. Next follow-up in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: PCP annual check-up, comprehensive medication review

notes.value:
Patient seen for annual physical and comprehensive medication review. Reports stable health. Mood has improved significantly with Sertraline. Orthostatic symptoms are well-controlled with Midodrine. Parkinson's motor symptoms are managed with his current regimen, with good 'on' times. No new falls since starting PT.

Reviewed all medications, ensuring no interactions and appropriate dosages. Labs today are generally stable. Discussed importance of bone health given age and fall risk (though currently lower risk with PT). Encouraged continued engagement with his specialist team. Renewed all prescriptions. Plan for routine follow-up in 6 months, and as needed.

chief_complaint.value: Routine Parkinson's Disease follow-up, disease progression monitoring

notes.value:
Routine 3-month follow-up for Parkinson's Disease. Patient reports generally stable motor symptoms. Non-motor symptoms (depression, orthostasis, constipation) are well-managed with current regimen. He continues physical therapy and reports maintaining an active lifestyle as much as possible.

Motor exam: Resting tremor remains present but mild, rigidity is minimal. Bradykinesia is present but stable. Postural stability has shown some improvement since starting PT. UPDRS motor score 24. Reviewed cognitive function with a brief assessment, which shows no significant decline. Discussed the long-term prognosis of PD and the importance of continued active management of both motor and non-motor symptoms. Plan for next follow-up in 3 months. No changes to medication regimen today.

outcome.value: Score 27/30 (normal for age and education)

summary.value: 33-year-old female initially presenting with anxiety, weight loss, and palpitations. Diagnosed with Graves' disease, developed agranulocytosis on methimazole, underwent radioactive iodine ablation, leading to subsequent hypothyroidism and worsening thyroid eye disease.

notes.value:
Patient Anika Patel, 33F, presents with a 2-month history of worsening anxiety, frequent palpitations, unintended 10lb weight loss, increased appetite, and fine hand tremor. She reports feeling constantly 'on edge' and has difficulty sleeping. History of generalized anxiety disorder managed previously with lifestyle changes. Denies chest pain or shortness of breath. 

On exam, she is tachycardic (HR 105 bpm), has a fine hand tremor, and mildly anxious affect. Thyroid gland diffusely enlarged, non-tender. Ordered comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC, TSH, Free T4, and TSI (Thyroid Stimulating Immunoglobulins) to evaluate for hyperthyroidism. Prescribed Lorazepam 0.5mg PRN for anxiety. Follow up in one week for lab review.

Patient returns for lab review. Labs confirm hyperthyroidism: TSH <0.01 uIU/mL (low), Free T4 3.5 ng/dL (high). TSI positive at 4.2 IU/L (elevated). CBC and CMP are normal. 

Diagnosis of Graves' disease confirmed. Started Methimazole 10mg twice daily. Counseled on medication adherence, potential side effects, and importance of monitoring for agranulocytosis (fever, sore throat). Referred to Dr. Rohan Mehta, Endocrinology, for specialized management and discussion of long-term treatment options. Referral letter sent.

New patient consult for Graves' disease. Reviewed PCP's workup and confirmed diagnosis. Patient reports mild improvement in palpitations and anxiety on Methimazole. Discussed treatment options including continued anti-thyroid drugs, radioactive iodine ablation, and thyroidectomy. Patient expresses interest in long-term solution. Counseled on risks/benefits of each. 

Plan to continue Methimazole 10mg BID. Will recheck thyroid function tests and CBC in 4 weeks. Instructed patient to report any fever, sore throat, or mouth sores immediately due to agranulocytosis risk. Follow-up to discuss definitive treatment after achieving euthyroid state.

Patient returns for follow-up. Reports significant improvement in hyperthyroid symptoms: weight stable, palpitations reduced, anxiety less severe. No fever or sore throat. 

Labs show improved thyroid function: TSH 0.05 uIU/mL (still suppressed but rising), Free T4 1.9 ng/dL (normalizing). CBC remains normal (WBC 6.5). Methimazole dose will be maintained. Discussed the possibility of radioactive iodine (RAI) ablation as a definitive treatment given the severity of her initial presentation and family history. Provided patient education material on RAI. Schedule for 4 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Fever and sore throat on Methimazole

notes.value:
Patient presented urgently to PCP with sudden onset of high fever (102.5F) and severe sore throat for 24 hours. She is visibly unwell. Denies rash or other symptoms. Concerned for Methimazole-induced agranulocytosis. 

Stat CBC ordered. Results showed severe leukopenia with WBC 1.5 K/uL and absolute neutrophil count (ANC) 0.3 K/uL (critical). Methimazole immediately discontinued. Patient admitted to hospital for IV antibiotics, neutropenic precautions, and G-CSF. Endocrinology consulted. Fortunately, patient recovered well without infection. Decision made to proceed with radioactive iodine ablation once stable.

Patient returns for pre-radioactive iodine consultation following resolution of agranulocytosis. ANC has fully recovered to 2.8 K/uL. Thyroid function is now slightly elevated again (Free T4 2.1) without Methimazole. 

Detailed counseling on radioactive iodine ablation procedure, including radiation precautions, potential for post-treatment hypothyroidism, and the risk of worsening thyroid eye disease. Patient is keen to proceed due to intolerance to Methimazole. Informed consent obtained. Scheduled for RAI treatment on June 20th. Instruction for low-iodine diet for 2 weeks prior.

Patient underwent radioactive iodine-131 ablation therapy for Graves' disease. Administered a single oral dose of 15 mCi I-131. Reiterated radiation safety precautions, including isolation from others, specific hygiene, and disposal procedures for 5-7 days. 

Advised on monitoring for symptoms of hypothyroidism in the coming weeks to months. Scheduled follow-up with endocrinology in 6 weeks to assess thyroid function post-RAI. Given information on how to recognize symptoms of evolving thyroid eye disease.

Patient returns 6 weeks post-RAI. Reports feeling slightly more tired than usual and mild dry eyes, but no major hyperthyroid symptoms. No fever or sore throat. 

Labs indicate persistent mild hyperthyroidism: TSH 0.08 uIU/mL (still suppressed), Free T4 1.9 ng/dL. This is expected as the RAI takes time to take full effect. Reassessed for thyroid eye disease, noted very subtle bilateral proptosis. Counseled on supportive eye care (artificial tears PRN). Recheck labs in 4 weeks. Continue to monitor for symptoms of hypothyroidism and worsening eye symptoms.

chief_complaint.value: New onset hypothyroidism symptoms, worsening eye symptoms

notes.value:
Patient reports significant fatigue, cold intolerance, and constipation over the past 2 weeks. Voice has become hoarser. Also notes increased eye dryness, irritation, and new mild diplopia (double vision). 

Labs confirm overt hypothyroidism: TSH 12.5 uIU/mL (high), Free T4 0.6 ng/dL (low). Started Levothyroxine 75mcg daily. Due to worsening eye symptoms, specifically diplopia and proptosis, referred to Dr. Elena Rodriguez, Ophthalmology, for evaluation of thyroid eye disease. Counseled on signs of severe TED requiring urgent attention.

New patient ophthalmology consult for thyroid eye disease. Patient reports bilateral proptosis, eye irritation, dryness, and intermittent diplopia. Visual acuity is 20/25 OU. Exophthalmometry measurements are 22mm OD, 21mm OS (normal <20mm). Lid lag present. Ocular motility exam reveals restrictive myopathy, primarily with upward gaze. 

Diagnosis: Active moderate-severe Thyroid Eye Disease. Initiated Artificial Tears PRN for dryness. Discussed potential for systemic corticosteroids to manage inflammation. Patient to start oral prednisone with a rheumatologist. Follow-up in 3 weeks to assess response to treatment and consider further intervention if needed.

Patient returns for Levothyroxine adjustment and to coordinate care for TED. Reports improvement in hypothyroid symptoms on Levothyroxine. Still experiencing significant eye irritation and diplopia as described to ophthalmology. 

Labs: TSH 3.2 uIU/mL (normalizing), Free T4 1.1 ng/dL (normal). Levothyroxine dose maintained. Started Prednisone 40mg daily, with a planned taper over 6-8 weeks, to address the active inflammatory phase of TED as recommended by ophthalmology. Counseled on steroid side effects. Continue close monitoring of thyroid function and eye symptoms.

Follow-up visit for TED, 3 weeks into prednisone course. Patient reports moderate improvement in eye irritation and pain. Diplopia is less frequent but still present, especially in extremes of gaze. Proptosis appears stable. Visual acuity remains 20/25 OU. 

Encouraged patient to continue prednisone taper as planned. Advised on wearing sunglasses outdoors and using cool compresses. Will continue to monitor for optic neuropathy. Next follow-up in 4 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Prednisone taper completion, overall thyroid status

notes.value:
Patient returns, having completed the prednisone taper for TED. Reports significant improvement in eye symptoms; dryness and irritation are minimal, and diplopia has largely resolved. Her hypothyroid symptoms are well-controlled on Levothyroxine. 

Labs: TSH 2.8 uIU/mL (stable), Free T4 1.3 ng/dL (stable). Prednisone officially discontinued. Discussed the importance of continued long-term follow-up for both hypothyroidism and TED. Referred to Dr. Liam O'Connell, Psychiatry, for ongoing anxiety management, as her thyroid-related stressors have largely resolved.

New patient consult for anxiety disorder. Patient reports feeling generally better after thyroid issues stabilized, but still experiences baseline anxiety, particularly with stress. Currently using Lorazepam 0.5mg PRN. Discussed therapeutic options including psychotherapy and potential for a daily SSRI. 

Patient is interested in exploring cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) first. Will continue Lorazepam PRN for now. Scheduled follow-up in 6 weeks to assess progress and reconsider medication if necessary. Provided resources for local CBT therapists.

Annual physical examination. Patient reports overall good health and satisfaction with current management of hypothyroidism and TED. Anxiety is manageable with PRN Lorazepam and she has started CBT. No new complaints. 

Reviewed medication list: Levothyroxine and Lorazepam are active. Reinforced adherence and importance of regular follow-ups with endocrinology and ophthalmology. Basic metabolic panel and CBC ordered as routine. Follow up as needed.

Routine endocrinology follow-up. Patient is euthyroid on current Levothyroxine dose. No lingering symptoms of hyper or hypothyroidism. Eye symptoms related to TED remain stable and well-controlled with artificial tears. No active inflammation. 

Labs: TSH 2.5 uIU/mL, Free T4 1.2 ng/dL (all stable and within target range). Plan: continue Levothyroxine 75mcg daily. Recheck thyroid function in 6 months or sooner if symptoms change. Emphasized importance of annual ophthalmology review for TED and regular PCP check-ups. Overall prognosis is excellent with continued management.

summary.value: 59-year-old male with a history of heavy alcohol use presenting with jaundice and ascites, diagnosed with cirrhosis and portal hypertension with esophageal varices. Experienced episodes of hepatic encephalopathy and is currently undergoing evaluation for liver transplant.

condition_043.label: Cirrhosis secondary to Alcohol-Related Liver Disease

name.value: Cirrhosis secondary to Alcohol-Related Liver Disease

chief_complaint.value: Yellow skin, abdominal swelling, fatigue for 2 months

notes.value: Mr. Rivera presents with progressive jaundice, increasing abdominal girth, and profound fatigue over the past 2 months. Reports a history of heavy alcohol consumption (approximately a fifth of liquor daily) for over 30 years, with recent reduction but not cessation. Denies acute pain, fever, or GI bleeding. Physical exam reveals icteric sclera, generalized jaundice, distended abdomen with shifting dullness suggestive of ascites, and spider angiomata on the chest. Liver edge palpable, firm. Initial labs ordered, including LFTs, bilirubin, CBC, INR, and kidney function. Urgent referral to Gastroenterology/Hepatology initiated.

chief_complaint.value: Hepatology consult for advanced liver disease

notes.value:
Consultation for Mr. Rivera, 59M, presenting with jaundice and ascites, with labs significant for elevated LFTs, hyperbilirubinemia, coagulopathy (INR 1.8), and hypoalbuminemia. History of chronic heavy alcohol use confirmed. Physical exam confirms signs of chronic liver disease. An ultrasound of the abdomen performed today shows diffuse coarse echotexture of the liver consistent with cirrhosis, splenomegaly, and large volume ascites.

Diagnosis: Cirrhosis secondary to Alcohol-Related Liver Disease with decompensation (ascites, coagulopathy). Initiated Furosemide 40mg daily and Spironolactone 100mg daily for ascites management. Advised strict alcohol cessation. Scheduled for upper endoscopy to screen for esophageal varices. Follow-up in 2 weeks for ascites and medication review.

outcome.value: Cirrhotic liver changes, splenomegaly, large volume ascites

notes.value:
Upper endoscopy performed today for screening of esophageal varices. Procedure revealed grade 2 esophageal varices with red whale signs, consistent with a high risk of bleeding. Four variceal bands were placed during the procedure to obliterate these high-risk varices.

Patient tolerated the procedure well with no immediate complications. Post-procedure instructions given, including proton pump inhibitor for 1 week and soft diet. Advised to avoid NSAIDs and alcohol. Scheduled follow-up with Dr. Bell in 1 week to discuss findings and long-term management.

outcome.value: Grade 2 esophageal varices banded

notes.value:
Follow-up after EGD. Patient reports mild throat soreness but no other complications. Discussed findings of grade 2 esophageal varices and successful banding. To prevent re-bleeding and progression, initiated Propranolol 20mg twice daily, titrating slowly. Re-emphasized the critical importance of absolute alcohol cessation. Ascites is improving with diuretics, weight down by 3 kg. Labs today show slight improvement in INR and bilirubin.

Plan: Continue diuretics and Propranolol. Recheck labs in 2 weeks. Next EGD for repeat banding/surveillance in 2 months. Continued strong recommendation for alcohol cessation. Referral to addiction specialist discussed.

Mr. Rivera returns for PCP follow-up. Ascites well controlled on current diuretic regimen. Reports occasional episodes of confusion or disorientation, particularly in the mornings, described by his family as 'sluggish thinking.' No overt tremor or asterixis noted on exam today, but he struggles with simple mental arithmetic. Family is concerned. Blood ammonia level ordered to rule out hepatic encephalopathy, given his cirrhosis.

Reviewed current medications. Emphasized sobriety. Will follow up with Dr. Bell regarding these new neurological symptoms and ammonia results. Continue current regimen, monitor for any worsening confusion.

Follow-up visit for Mr. Rivera with new reports of episodic confusion. Reviewed ammonia level of 65 umol/L from PCP. Based on clinical picture and elevated ammonia, diagnosis of Hepatic Encephalopathy (HE) is made. Initiated Lactulose 30ml orally twice daily, titrating dose to achieve 2-3 soft bowel movements per day. Patient and family educated on signs of worsening HE and importance of medication adherence. Emphasized again the need for strict alcohol abstinence.

Ascites remains well controlled. Propranolol tolerated. Scheduled for repeat EGD in January. MELD score calculated at 15. Discussed potential for liver transplant evaluation in the future, stressing the absolute requirement for sustained sobriety.

Patient presented to ED with acute worsening of confusion and disorientation, brought by family. Family reports he missed several doses of Lactulose. On arrival, patient was disoriented to time and place, drowsy, with mild asterixis. Ammonia level drawn in ED was 110 umol/L. Admitted for acute hepatic encephalopathy management.

Treatment included IV fluids, increased dose of oral Lactulose, and close neurological monitoring. Ammonia levels slowly trended down. After 2 days, patient's mental status returned to baseline. Discharged with strict instructions for Lactulose adherence and follow-up with Hepatology.

Follow-up post-ED visit for acute HE. Patient's mental status has returned to baseline. Reviewed hospital course and emphasized the critical importance of Lactulose adherence. Due to recurrent HE, decided to add Rifaximin 550mg twice daily to his regimen for secondary prophylaxis. Patient educated on the purpose of Rifaximin and potential side effects.

Confirmed strict sobriety since hospital discharge. Liver function tests show stable but still elevated bilirubin (5.8 mg/dL). Next EGD scheduled next week. MELD score will be reassessed after EGD and sustained stability. Continued counseling on alcohol cessation.

Repeat EGD performed. Exam showed persistent, but smaller, grade 1 esophageal varices. No active bleeding or new red whale signs. No further banding required at this time. Gastric varices were not seen. Patient tolerated the procedure well.

Findings are reassuring, indicating stability of variceal disease with Propranolol therapy. Will continue current management. Next surveillance EGD in 1 year unless symptoms dictate earlier. Re-emphasized continued medication adherence and sobriety.

outcome.value: Grade 1 esophageal varices, no further banding needed

notes.value:
Mr. Rivera returns for PCP follow-up. Reports feeling significantly better with mental clarity improved on Lactulose and Rifaximin. He is committed to sobriety and has remained abstinent from alcohol since his ED visit. He is actively seeking support groups.

Referred him to Dr. Emily White, an addiction psychiatrist, for formal assessment and ongoing support for alcohol cessation. Reinforced the benefits of sobriety for his liver health and potential transplant candidacy. Labs today show stable liver function. BP and HR within normal limits on Propranolol. Ascites remains well controlled.

Consultation for Mr. Rivera regarding alcohol use disorder. Patient expresses strong motivation for sobriety due to his liver disease. Has been abstinent for 7 weeks. Discussed triggers and coping strategies. Will engage in regular individual therapy sessions and encouraged participation in a local support group. No immediate pharmacotherapy for alcohol use disorder initiated given his current commitment and stable mental status, but will be monitored closely.

Reviewed the importance of sobriety for transplant candidacy. Patient is fully aware of the implications. Scheduled for bi-weekly follow-up sessions.

chief_complaint.value: Hepatology follow-up, MELD update, transplant discussion

notes.value:
Routine hepatology follow-up. Mr. Rivera reports continued excellent adherence to medications and sustained alcohol sobriety (now 4 months). No episodes of HE. Ascites remains well controlled. Current labs show stable liver function, bilirubin 3.9 mg/dL, INR 1.3, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, sodium 134 mEq/L. MELD score is 18.

Given sustained sobriety and stable condition, initiated formal referral to the Liver Transplant team for evaluation. Patient counseled on the rigorous process and requirements. Continue current medical regimen and follow-up with Dr. White for sobriety support. Next hepatology follow-up in 2 months.

Initial evaluation for liver transplant. Comprehensive review of Mr. Rivera's medical history, including alcohol-related cirrhosis, esophageal varices, and recurrent hepatic encephalopathy. Confirmed 5 months of documented alcohol sobriety. All medical records reviewed.

Patient underwent baseline labs, imaging (CT abdomen, chest X-ray), and cardiac evaluation (ECG, echo). Initial assessment finds him medically suitable for transplant evaluation. Referred for formal psychosocial assessment by transplant social worker and psychologist. Patient educated on transplant process, waitlist, and post-transplant care. Next visit after completion of all assessments.

outcome.value: Cirrhotic liver morphology, splenomegaly, no hepatocellular carcinoma
Normal cardiac function, no pulmonary hypertension

notes.value:
Psychosocial assessment for liver transplant candidacy. Mr. Rivera has maintained 6 months of continuous alcohol sobriety, verified by collateral and urine toxicology screens. He demonstrates a strong understanding of his disease and commitment to lifelong sobriety and medication adherence. He has good social support from his family. He has engaged consistently in individual therapy sessions. No active psychiatric contraindications to transplant identified.

Recommended for transplant listing from a psychosocial perspective. Will continue to follow him for ongoing sobriety support.

Mr. Rivera's case was presented to the Liver Transplant Listing Committee today. All required evaluations (medical, psychiatric, social) have been completed and are favorable. He has met the mandatory 6-month sobriety period. MELD score calculated at 19.

The committee approved Mr. Rivera for liver transplant listing. Patient was informed and expressed gratitude. He is now officially on the waiting list. Provided detailed instructions for remaining on the list, including quarterly clinic visits, monthly labs, and contact information for the transplant coordinator. Emphasized immediate notification for any changes in health status or new symptoms.

chief_complaint.value: Quarterly Hepatology follow-up, transplant waitlist management

notes.value:
Quarterly follow-up for Mr. Rivera, who is now listed for liver transplant. Patient remains clinically stable. No recent episodes of HE or ascites exacerbation. Reports continued sobriety. Physical exam is stable.

Labs today: Total Bilirubin 3.5 mg/dL, INR 1.2, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, Sodium 135 mEq/L. MELD score calculated at 17. These values are stable and reflect ongoing management. Reinforced the importance of diet, adherence to medications, and communication with the transplant team. Next follow-up in 3 months with repeat labs.

chief_complaint.value: Annual physical and transplant waitlist review

notes.value:
Annual physical for Mr. Rivera. Overall health remains stable. Patient is active on the liver transplant waiting list and continues to adhere strictly to all medical and sobriety requirements. No new complaints. BP 128/78, HR 68. Lungs clear, cardiac exam normal. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no significant ascites noted. 

Reviewed recent labs from Hepatology (MELD 17, stable). Discussed flu shot and updated vaccinations. Emphasized maintaining healthy lifestyle and continued communication with all his specialists. Reviewed mental health and ongoing sobriety support with Dr. White. All systems are stable while awaiting transplant.

summary.value: 55-year-old female presenting with symptoms of generalized anxiety and major depressive disorder, managed with SSRI therapy and psychotherapy referrals.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent fatigue, difficulty sleeping, constant worry

notes.value: Patient is a 55-year-old female presenting with a 3-month history of persistent fatigue, insomnia with difficulty falling and staying asleep, and a pervasive sense of worry. She reports feeling overwhelmed and tearful, with decreased interest in hobbies she once enjoyed. Symptoms started after a major company restructuring and recent job insecurity. Denies suicidal ideation, hallucinations. Physical exam unremarkable. Vitals stable. Initial workup to rule out organic causes planned.

chief_complaint.value: Lab review, ongoing anxiety and low mood

notes.value: Patient returns for lab review. CBC, CMP, TSH, Vitamin D all within normal limits, ruling out common physical causes for fatigue and mood disturbance. Symptoms persist; GAD-7 score 18 (severe anxiety), PHQ-9 score 20 (severe depression). Discussed preliminary diagnosis of Generalized Anxiety Disorder and Major Depressive Disorder. Prescribed Lorazepam 0.5mg PRN for acute anxiety attacks. Discussed starting an SSRI at next visit after patient considers options. Recommended beginning a mood diary.

chief_complaint.value: Decision to start antidepressant, review options

notes.value: Patient has decided to proceed with antidepressant medication. Discussed Sertraline, its mechanism, potential side effects, and titration schedule. Started Sertraline 50mg daily. Counseled on delayed onset of therapeutic effects and potential initial worsening of anxiety. Reviewed strategies for managing initial side effects. Emphasized importance of adherence and follow-up. Also provided referral for psychiatric evaluation and CBT. Encouraged continued use of PRN Lorazepam if anxiety becomes unmanageable.

Initial psychiatric consultation for Linda Morrison. Patient has been on Sertraline 50mg for 3 weeks. Reports some initial GI upset and increased anxiety for the first few days, which has now subsided. Mood is slightly improved, but anxiety remains significant, particularly with job search. GAD-7 score 16, PHQ-9 score 17. No suicidal ideation. Diagnostic impression confirmed: MDD, recurrent, moderate; GAD. Reviewed current medication plan. Will continue Sertraline 50mg for now and consider dose increase if no further improvement in 2 weeks. Encouraged engaging with CBT. Follow-up in 3 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: PCP check-in, medication adherence, general well-being

notes.value: PCP follow-up to assess overall progress. Patient reports feeling 'a little better' on Sertraline, but still struggles with persistent low-level anxiety and occasional depressive thoughts. She has started CBT sessions with Dr. Olivia Wilson and finds them somewhat helpful. Has used Lorazepam sparingly, mostly for specific stressful events. Given the ongoing but mild improvement, advised to continue current Sertraline dose and CBT, with upcoming psychiatry follow-up to potentially adjust medication. Discontinued Lorazepam, encouraging reliance on coping skills from CBT.

Follow-up psychiatric visit. Patient reports some improvement in mood and anxiety, but still experiencing significant intrusive thoughts and anticipatory worry, especially related to financial stability. GAD-7 score 14, PHQ-9 score 15. She is tolerating Sertraline 50mg well with no significant side effects. Agreed to increase Sertraline to 100mg daily to achieve better symptom control. Counseled on possibility of transient side effects with dose increase. Will continue CBT. Recheck in 4-6 weeks.

Routine follow-up 6 weeks after Sertraline dosage increase to 100mg. Patient reports significant improvement in both mood and anxiety. She feels less overwhelmed, is sleeping better, and has resumed interest in several hobbies. GAD-7 score 8 (mild anxiety), PHQ-9 score 9 (mild depression). No side effects reported. Encouraged continued CBT. Discussed long-term management and importance of adherence. Labs ordered for routine metabolic panel given long-term SSRI use. Recheck in 3 months.

summary.value: 62-year-old male with a history of recurrent gout flares and newly diagnosed chronic kidney disease stage 2, managed with allopurinol, lisinopril, and dietary modifications.

chief_complaint.value: Acute severe pain and swelling in left great toe

notes.value: Patient, a 62-year-old male, presents with sudden onset excruciating pain, swelling, and redness in the left great toe since yesterday morning. Denies trauma. Describes similar self-resolving episodes in the past. Physical exam reveals a hot, swollen, tender left first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint, exquisitely painful to touch. No signs of infection. Labs show elevated uric acid (8.5 mg/dL). Diagnosis of acute gout flare. Prescribed colchicine for acute management. Advised NSAIDs are contraindicated due to age and potential renal concerns. Recommended follow-up in two weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Follow-up on gout flare, general check-up

notes.value: Patient returns for follow-up regarding the acute gout flare. Reports significant improvement in left great toe pain and swelling with colchicine; no residual pain or tenderness. Discussed long-term management of gout. Initiated allopurinol 100mg daily to lower uric acid levels and prevent future flares. Ordered a comprehensive metabolic panel and repeat uric acid to monitor treatment efficacy and kidney function. Advised on dietary changes, including limiting purine-rich foods and alcohol. Referral to nephrology placed due to new finding of elevated creatinine.

Patient referred for evaluation of persistently elevated creatinine levels identified by PCP. History includes recent gout diagnosis. Reviewed previous labs showing creatinine 1.5 mg/dL and eGFR 55 mL/min/1.73m2. Patient denies specific symptoms of renal impairment such as edema or changes in urination. No history of diabetes or uncontrolled hypertension. Physical exam is unremarkable for overt signs of kidney disease. Urinalysis shows trace protein. Diagnosis of Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 2. Initiated Lisinopril 5mg daily for renal protection and blood pressure management. Advised on low-sodium diet and adequate fluid intake. Follow-up in 4 weeks for lab recheck.

Patient returns for routine follow-up. Reports a mild flare of gout in the right ankle, which started 2 days ago. Pain is rated 3/10, improving with rest. Continues allopurinol and lisinopril without reported issues. Blood pressure 130/80 mmHg. Physical exam shows mild tenderness in the right ankle, with no significant swelling or erythema. Prescribed a short course of colchicine for acute flare management (0.6mg BID for 3 days). Reinforced the importance of adherence to allopurinol and ongoing dietary recommendations to prevent future episodes.

Follow-up visit with nephrology for ongoing management of CKD Stage 2. Patient reports no new symptoms or complications. Tolerating lisinopril well, blood pressure is well controlled at 126/76 mmHg. Reviewed recent labs showing stable creatinine at 1.4 mg/dL and eGFR 58 mL/min/1.73m2. Potassium level is 4.3 mEq/L, within normal limits. Urinalysis continues to show trace protein but no worsening. Discussed the ongoing importance of medication adherence, a low-sodium diet, and maintaining adequate hydration to preserve renal function. Continue current regimen. Next follow-up in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: Annual physical exam, gout and CKD check

notes.value: Patient presents for his annual physical examination. Reports no gout flares since the last mild episode in June. Feeling well overall, with no new concerning symptoms. Continues both allopurinol and lisinopril as prescribed. Review of systems is stable. Physical exam reveals no active joint inflammation or edema. Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg. Labs from today show controlled uric acid at 5.8 mg/dL and stable kidney function with creatinine 1.4 mg/dL and eGFR 57 mL/min/1.73m2. Continue current management plan. Advised on seasonal influenza vaccination. Schedule routine follow-up in 6 months.

summary.value: 34-year-old G1P0 female diagnosed with Gestational Diabetes in the second trimester, requiring insulin for glycemic control. Later developed Preeclampsia with severe features requiring delivery.

notes.value:
34-year-old G1P0 patient presenting for her first prenatal visit at 8 weeks gestation. Confirmed pregnancy with home test. Last menstrual period (LMP) 2024-01-20. Reports mild nausea and fatigue. Medical history otherwise unremarkable. Social history: non-smoker, occasional alcohol pre-pregnancy, denies illicit drug use. Discussed routine prenatal care, healthy diet, and lifestyle modifications.

Ordered comprehensive prenatal labs including CBC, blood type and screen, RPR, HIV, Hepatitis B and C, Rubella, Varicella, Urinalysis. Started on Prenatal Vitamins. Scheduled for follow-up in 4 weeks.

Patient returns for review of initial prenatal labs. All labs within normal limits, including CBC, infectious disease panel, and urinalysis. Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, weight 135 lbs. Fetal heart tones audible at 140 bpm. Patient continues to experience mild nausea but it is improving. Denies any bleeding or contractions. Discussed genetic screening options. Scheduled for anatomy scan at 20 weeks and glucose screening at 28 weeks.

Patient presents for 28-week routine prenatal visit. Reports feeling well, fetal movements active. Blood pressure 115/75 mmHg. Fundal height 28 cm. No edema. Discussed third-trimester discomforts and warning signs. Ordered 1-hour glucose tolerance test (GTT) for gestational diabetes screening, per protocol. Reviewed results of 20-week anatomy scan which was normal. Scheduled for GTT and follow-up lab review.

chief_complaint.value: Glucose Tolerance Test results and Gestational Diabetes diagnosis

notes.value:
Patient called in for urgent review of GTT results. 1-hour GTT was elevated, requiring a 3-hour GTT. The 3-hour GTT results were: Fasting 98 mg/dL (normal <95), 1-hr 195 mg/dL (normal <180), 2-hr 165 mg/dL (normal <155), 3-hr 142 mg/dL (normal <140). Two values elevated, confirming diagnosis of Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM).

Patient counseled on GDM diagnosis, dietary modifications, and importance of blood glucose monitoring. Referred to a nutritionist for education and meal planning. Will start with diet and exercise modifications; if blood sugars remain uncontrolled, insulin therapy will be considered. Scheduled for weekly follow-up visits.

Patient has been monitoring blood glucose for 2 weeks. Despite adherence to dietary recommendations from nutritionist, fasting blood sugars remain elevated (averaging 105-110 mg/dL). Post-prandial readings also frequently exceed target. Fetal growth scan shows fundal height tracking large for gestational age.

Discussed need for insulin therapy to achieve optimal glycemic control and prevent macrosomia. Initiated Insulin Aspart (Novolog) with a starting dose regimen, and educated on proper injection technique and hypoglycemic precautions. Continue close monitoring of blood glucose and weekly prenatal visits. Referred to Maternal-Fetal Medicine for co-management.

Patient at 35 weeks gestation. GDM well-controlled on insulin. However, today's blood pressure is 145/95 mmHg, repeat 148/92 mmHg (previously normotensive). Patient reports mild headache and some swelling in her hands and feet. No visual changes or epigastric pain. Urinalysis dipstick shows 2+ protein. Given new onset hypertension with proteinuria in late pregnancy, strong suspicion for Preeclampsia.

Ordered comprehensive Preeclampsia labs (CBC, CMP, LDH, Uric Acid, 24-hour urine protein) and fetal non-stress test. Discussed warning signs of preeclampsia and advised immediate return for any severe symptoms. Will review labs urgently.

chief_complaint.value: Lab review, Preeclampsia diagnosis, and management

notes.value:
Patient presented to Labor & Delivery for severe headache and blurred vision. Repeat BP 155/98 mmHg. Review of labs from previous visit and current: Platelets 120,000 (low, ref >150,000), AST 78 U/L (high, ref <35), ALT 85 U/L (high, ref <35), 24-hour urine protein 850 mg (high, ref <300). Fetal non-stress test reactive. These findings confirm diagnosis of Preeclampsia with severe features.

Admitted for inpatient management. Started on Labetalol for blood pressure control. Discussed induction of labor given gestational age (35+ weeks) and severe features of preeclampsia. Informed patient about risks and benefits. Plan for delivery within 24-48 hours.

Patient discharged postpartum after successful delivery of a healthy term infant on 2024-11-12. Preeclampsia symptoms resolved postpartum; blood pressure stabilized to 120/80 mmHg without medication. Gestational Diabetes also resolved, no longer requiring insulin. Patient advised to discontinue Labetalol and Insulin.

Discussed postpartum care, warning signs, and follow-up plan. Recommended 6-week postpartum check-up. Counselled on increased risk for Type 2 Diabetes after GDM and future risk of preeclampsia in subsequent pregnancies. Discharged home in stable condition.

summary.value: 48-year-old male with chronic obesity and newly diagnosed obstructive sleep apnea, presenting with fatigue and snoring. Undergoing CPAP therapy and managing dyslipidemia with medication and lifestyle changes.

notes.value: Patient is a 48-year-old male presenting with a 6-month history of worsening fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, and reports of very loud snoring from his partner. Denies leg swelling, chest pain, or recent illness. BMI is 32.5 kg/m^2. Social history notable for sedentary lifestyle and occasional alcohol use. Given symptoms and body habitus, high suspicion for Obstructive Sleep Apnea. Ordered comprehensive labs including CBC, CMP, TSH, and a full lipid panel. Counselled on healthy diet and exercise.

chief_complaint.value: Lab review; persistent snoring and fatigue

notes.value: Patient returns for lab review. CBC, CMP, and TSH are all within normal limits. Lipid panel shows elevated Total Cholesterol (235 mg/dL), LDL (155 mg/dL), and Triglycerides (180 mg/dL), confirming dyslipidemia. Patient reports no change in snoring or fatigue. Given his symptoms and the high suspicion for OSA, a formal referral to a sleep specialist (Dr. David Lee) was made for further evaluation and a sleep study. Discussed initial steps for diet modification.

chief_complaint.value: Sleep medicine consultation for suspected OSA

notes.value: New patient consultation for evaluation of suspected Obstructive Sleep Apnea. Reviewed PCP notes and patient history of loud snoring, daytime fatigue, and unrefreshing sleep. Physical exam reveals a crowded oropharynx (Mallampati score III) and increased neck circumference. Discussed risks of untreated OSA including cardiovascular complications. Explained the polysomnography procedure. Scheduled an in-lab sleep study to confirm diagnosis and determine severity.

outcome.value: Scheduled for 2024-04-20

chief_complaint.value: Review sleep study results; OSA diagnosis and CPAP initiation

notes.value: Follow-up visit to review results of polysomnography. Sleep study revealed an Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) of 35 events per hour, with significant oxygen desaturations. This confirms a diagnosis of severe Obstructive Sleep Apnea. Discussed CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure) as the primary treatment. Patient expressed understanding and willingness to start therapy. CPAP machine ordered, and patient was scheduled for an education session with a respiratory therapist for device setup and mask fitting. Encouraged continued weight management efforts.

outcome.value: Scheduled for 2024-05-08

chief_complaint.value: PCP follow-up after OSA diagnosis and CPAP initiation; dyslipidemia management

notes.value: Patient reports he started CPAP therapy one week ago and is already noticing some improvement in daytime alertness and reduced snoring. Adherence is good so far. Discussed the importance of weight loss in managing OSA and overall health. Given persistent dyslipidemia and lack of significant lifestyle changes yet, decided to initiate Rosuvastatin 10mg daily. Referred to Dr. Maya Patel for specialized nutrition counseling to support weight management. Encouraged regular exercise.

chief_complaint.value: Routine check-up, CPAP adherence, and dyslipidemia follow-up

notes.value: Patient presents for routine follow-up. Reports significant improvement in fatigue and sleep quality since starting CPAP, with excellent adherence. Partner confirms snoring has almost completely resolved. He has lost 5 lbs since last visit through dietary changes recommended by Dr. Patel. Rosuvastatin is well-tolerated with no side effects. Rechecked lipid panel to assess medication efficacy. Reinforced continued adherence to CPAP and lifestyle modifications. Next follow-up in 3 months.

summary.value: 71-year-old female with long-standing essential hypertension and progressive bilateral knee osteoarthritis, presenting for management of knee pain and evaluation for surgical intervention.

chief_complaint.value: Routine check-up, managing hypertension, new knee pain

notes.value:
Patient presents for annual physical. Long-standing essential hypertension currently well-controlled on Lisinopril 10mg daily. Recent blood pressures have been within target range. New complaint of mild, intermittent pain in both knees, worse with activity and stairs, for the past 3 months. Denies trauma, swelling, or redness. No fever.

Physical exam reveals mild crepitus in both knees with flexion, no effusion or warmth. Cardiovascular exam normal. Labs ordered including CBC, CMP, and lipid panel. Advised continued Lisinopril, and started Acetaminophen for knee pain as needed. Follow up in 1 month for lab review.

Patient returns for follow-up. Lab results from last visit were all within normal limits. However, she reports that her bilateral knee pain has worsened significantly over the past month, despite regular use of acetaminophen. Pain is now rated 7/10 at its worst, interfering with daily activities such as walking and climbing stairs. Occasional stiffness, particularly in the mornings, lasting for about 30 minutes.

Exam shows bilateral knee tenderness along medial joint lines, with mild crepitus and pain on full flexion. No warmth, redness, or significant effusion. Range of motion limited by pain. Given the progression of symptoms, X-rays of both knees were ordered to evaluate for osteoarthritis. Advised to continue acetaminophen and avoid aggravating activities. Will review X-ray results when available.

Patient returns to review X-ray results of both knees. Imaging reveals severe tricompartmental osteoarthritis in both knees, with significant joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. Findings are consistent with advanced degenerative joint disease.

Discussed findings with patient and management options. Given the severity of pain and imaging findings, initiated Meloxicam 15mg daily as a trial for inflammatory pain relief, with close monitoring for blood pressure or GI side effects. Referral to Orthopedic Surgery (Dr. Robert Lee) for further evaluation and discussion of potential surgical interventions was provided. Patient understands the chronic nature of her condition and the need for specialist input.

outcome.value: Severe tricompartmental osteoarthritis with joint space narrowing and osteophytes.

notes.value:
Patient seen for orthopedic consultation regarding severe bilateral knee osteoarthritis. Reviewed PCP notes and imaging. Patient reports moderate relief with Meloxicam, but pain remains significant, especially in the right knee. Exam confirms severe degenerative changes, with marked crepitus, pain on palpation, and limited range of motion bilaterally. Right knee pain is slightly more dominant.

Discussed conservative management options including physical therapy, bracing, and injections. Offered corticosteroid injection for symptomatic relief in the right knee. Surgical options, including total knee arthroplasty, were discussed in detail, emphasizing risks and benefits. Patient elected for a right knee injection today and will consider surgical options in the future. Follow up in 6-8 weeks.

outcome.value: Injection administered without complication for pain relief.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent knee pain, mild blood pressure elevation

notes.value:
Patient returns reporting that the right knee injection provided about 3 weeks of good relief, but pain has returned. Meloxicam provided some relief but she noticed her home blood pressure readings were slightly elevated since starting it (averaging 145/85 mmHg, up from her usual 125/75 mmHg). No other side effects.

Blood pressure checked in office today: 148/86 mmHg. Given the potential impact of NSAIDs on blood pressure, discontinued Meloxicam. To maintain hypertension control, added Amlodipine 5mg daily. Encouraged continued acetaminophen for pain. Discussed the need to revisit surgical options with orthopedics if conservative measures continue to fail. Patient advised to monitor BP at home and report any concerns.

chief_complaint.value: Discuss total knee replacement for right knee

notes.value:
Patient returns to Orthopedics after attempting conservative management, including a steroid injection which offered temporary relief. She reports that her right knee pain continues to be debilitating and is significantly impacting her quality of life and mobility. She has decided she wishes to proceed with total knee replacement for the right knee.

Reviewed the surgical procedure, recovery, and potential complications again. Patient is fully informed and wishes to move forward. Initiated pre-operative workup including blood tests and a cardiology consult for clearance given her history of hypertension. Scheduled surgery for late October. Follow-up with PCP for pre-operative clearance.

outcome.value: Tests ordered for surgical clearance.

chief_complaint.value: Pre-operative clearance for right total knee replacement

notes.value:
Patient presents for pre-operative medical clearance for right total knee replacement scheduled for October. Reviewed recent cardiology consult note (Dr. Anya Sharma) which cleared her for surgery, noting stable hypertension on Lisinopril and Amlodipine. Denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or new cardiac symptoms.

Pre-operative labs are within acceptable limits. CBC shows adequate hemoglobin. Renal function is stable. Reviewed medications and advised which ones to hold prior to surgery per orthopedic and anesthesia instructions. Patient is well-prepared for surgery. Clearance provided for total knee replacement.

summary.value: 40-year-old male presenting with chronic diarrhea, rectal bleeding, and abdominal pain, diagnosed with Ulcerative Colitis, complicated by Iron Deficiency Anemia, managed with mesalamine and iron supplementation.

notes.value:
Patient presents with a 2-month history of increasing stool frequency (4-5 loose stools/day), occasional rectal bleeding, and intermittent lower abdominal cramping. Denies fever, nausea, vomiting, or significant weight loss. Reports fatigue. No recent travel or dietary changes. Family history notable for father with Crohn's Disease.

Physical exam reveals mild diffuse abdominal tenderness. No rebound or guarding. Digital rectal exam showed trace heme-positive stool. Ordered CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP, and stool studies (culture, C. diff, ova & parasites). Advised clear liquids for 24 hours and return for lab review.

Patient returns for lab review. CBC shows microcytic anemia (Hgb 10.5 g/dL, MCV 75 fL). ESR 35 mm/hr and CRP 1.8 mg/dL are both elevated. Stool studies negative for infection. Given the chronicity of symptoms, rectal bleeding, anemia, and elevated inflammatory markers, concern for inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is high.

Discussed findings with the patient. Explained the need for specialist evaluation. Initiated referral to Gastroenterology for further workup, including colonoscopy. Counseled on increasing dietary iron intake and monitoring symptoms.

New patient gastroenterology consultation for chronic diarrhea and rectal bleeding. Reviewed PCP notes, labs (anemia, elevated ESR/CRP), and negative stool studies. Patient reports ongoing symptoms, though bleeding has lessened slightly. Physical exam reveals mild abdominal tenderness in the left lower quadrant.

Discussed differential diagnosis, including Ulcerative Colitis and Crohn's Disease. Recommended full colonoscopy with ileal intubation and biopsies to confirm diagnosis and assess disease extent. Pre-procedure instructions provided. Will follow up after the procedure to discuss findings and initiate treatment.

Follow-up post-colonoscopy. Colonoscopy revealed continuous inflammation extending from the rectum through the entire colon, with loss of vascular pattern, erythema, friability, and erosions, consistent with pancolitis. Terminal ileum was normal. Biopsies confirmed chronic active colitis with crypt architectural distortion and increased plasma cells, confirming Ulcerative Colitis.

Discussed diagnosis and treatment options. Initiated oral Mesalamine 1.2g three times daily. Counseled on medication adherence, potential side effects, and diet modifications. Will re-evaluate symptoms and labs in approximately 4 weeks. Referred patient back to PCP for ongoing anemia management.

outcome.value: Pancolitis with mucosal inflammation and erosions, biopsies confirm Ulcerative Colitis.

notes.value:
Patient reports significant improvement in GI symptoms since starting Mesalamine 3 weeks ago. Stool frequency reduced to 2-3 formed stools per day, and rectal bleeding has ceased. Abdominal pain is minimal. However, fatigue persists.

Repeat labs show Hgb 10.8 g/dL, Ferritin 12 ng/mL (low), Iron 30 mcg/dL (low). Anemia remains despite symptom improvement. Initiated Ferrous Sulfate 325mg daily. Emphasized importance of consistent medication intake for both UC and anemia. Follow-up in 6 weeks to assess iron repletion and UC control.

Routine PCP follow-up. Patient reports feeling significantly better overall. UC symptoms remain well-controlled with Mesalamine. Fatigue has improved with iron supplementation. No new GI complaints. Tolerating both medications well.

Repeat CBC shows Hgb 12.1 g/dL, Ferritin 45 ng/mL. Anemia is resolving. Advised to continue Ferrous Sulfate for another month and recheck. Reinforced importance of regular follow-ups with gastroenterology and adherence to medication regimen. Will coordinate care with Dr. Lee for ongoing UC management.

chief_complaint.value: Quarterly GI follow-up, UC in remission

notes.value:
Quarterly follow-up with Gastroenterology. Patient reports sustained remission of Ulcerative Colitis. No diarrhea, bleeding, or abdominal pain. Eating well, energy levels normal. Has been consistent with Mesalamine and Ferrous Sulfate.

Physical exam unremarkable. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Repeat labs show Hgb 13.0 g/dL, CRP <0.5 mg/dL. Both within normal limits. Discussed long-term management strategies. Plan to continue Mesalamine 1.2g TID indefinitely for maintenance. Continue Ferrous Sulfate for one more month, then discontinue if follow-up labs confirm sustained iron levels. Next GI follow-up in 3 months.

summary.value: 29-year-old female with a history of chronic migraines with aura, managed with topiramate, and concurrent anxiety disorder receiving therapy.

chief_complaint.value: Frequent severe headaches with visual aura for 6 months

notes.value: Patient is a 29-year-old female presenting with a 6-month history of increasingly frequent and severe headaches. Describes throbbing pain, typically unilateral, preceded by shimmering lights or zig-zag patterns (aura) lasting 20-30 minutes. Headaches last 4-72 hours, rated 8/10, associated with nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. Occur 3-4 times per month. No focal neurological deficits on exam. Provisional diagnosis of Migraine with Aura. Advised symptomatic relief with ibuprofen and referred to Neurology for further evaluation and preventive strategies.

chief_complaint.value: Neurology consult for chronic migraines with aura

notes.value: New patient consultation for chronic migraines with aura. Reviewed detailed headache diary provided by patient, consistent with diagnostic criteria for Migraine with Aura. Discussed lifestyle modifications, abortive therapies, and preventive medications. Given frequency and severity, initiated Topiramate, starting at 25mg daily for one week, then increasing to 25mg twice daily for another week, with target titration to 50mg twice daily. Counselled on potential side effects including paresthesias, cognitive slowing, and appetite changes. Scheduled follow-up in 6 weeks to assess efficacy and tolerability.

chief_complaint.value: Follow-up on migraine treatment, increasing anxiety

notes.value: Patient returns for follow-up after starting Topiramate for migraines. Reports experiencing mild paresthesias in hands and feet and some difficulty with word-finding, but these are tolerable. Migraine frequency has slightly reduced to 2-3 per month. However, she expresses increasing anxiety about daily tasks and future health, reporting feeling constantly on edge and having difficulty relaxing. Denies panic attacks or suicidal ideation. Ordered basic labs (CBC, CMP, TSH, Vitamin D) to rule out any underlying contributors to anxiety and fatigue. Advised to continue Topiramate and track anxiety symptoms. Will review labs and discuss anxiety management further.

chief_complaint.value: Neurology follow-up for migraine management, Topiramate efficacy

notes.value: Follow-up neurology visit. Patient is tolerating Topiramate 50mg twice daily with stable side effects (mild paresthesias, occasional word-finding difficulty). Migraine frequency has decreased further to 1-2 per month, with reduced intensity. Reports some improvement in overall quality of life regarding headaches. Discussed continued adherence to medication. Patient also mentioned ongoing anxiety, which she plans to discuss further with her PCP. No changes to migraine regimen at this time. Scheduled follow-up in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent anxiety, review of recent labs

notes.value: Patient returns to discuss persistent anxiety. Reviewed labs from previous visit, all within normal limits, ruling out obvious medical causes for anxiety symptoms. Patient describes significant impact of anxiety on work and social life, including difficulty sleeping and constant worry. Patient meets criteria for Generalized Anxiety Disorder. Discussed treatment options, including cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) and medication. Patient preferred to start with therapy. Provided referral to Ms. Olivia Green for psychotherapy. Also reviewed recent neurology visit and noted good migraine control. Emphasized importance of adherence to Topiramate.

chief_complaint.value: Routine check-up, update on anxiety and migraines

notes.value: Routine follow-up. Patient reports significant improvement in anxiety symptoms since starting therapy with Ms. Green. Expresses feeling more in control and better equipped to manage stress. Migraines remain well-controlled on Topiramate, occurring less than once a month and are milder. Continues to track symptoms. Advised to continue therapy and Topiramate. Encouraged regular exercise and mindfulness practices. Scheduled annual physical for next year, and referral to neurology will be maintained as needed.

summary.value: 66-year-old male with a history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, presenting with worsening glycemic control and new onset peripheral neuropathy, leading to insulin initiation and ongoing management.

notes.value: Patient is a 66-year-old male with established history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Hypertension, and Hyperlipidemia. Presents for routine annual physical. Reports feeling generally well but admits to occasional fatigue and thirst. States he is adherent to current medications (Metformin, Lisinopril, Simvastatin) but struggles with consistent diet and exercise. Physical exam shows BP 138/85, HR 72, BMI 29. No acute distress. Ordered comprehensive labs including HbA1c, lipid panel, kidney and liver function tests, and urinalysis. Advised dietary modifications and increased activity. Will review labs in 1 week.

chief_complaint.value: Review of lab results and medication adherence
