notes.value: Patient returns to review labs. HbA1c is 8.2% (elevated from 7.5% 6 months prior), LDL 115 mg/dL (above target). Kidney function stable. BP in clinic today 135/82. Discussed implications of elevated HbA1c and LDL, reinforcing importance of diet, exercise, and medication adherence. Increased Metformin dosage. Patient expressed concern about fatigue and mild tingling in his feet, especially at night. Reassured and advised to monitor symptoms. Scheduled follow-up in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent fatigue, increased thirst, new numbness/tingling in feet

notes.value: Patient returns expressing concerns about persistent fatigue and increased thirst/urination despite increased Metformin dose. More notably, he reports worsening numbness and tingling in both feet, described as a 'pins and needles' sensation, particularly at night, interfering with sleep. Symptoms are bilateral and symmetrical, ascending slightly above the ankles. Denies acute pain, weakness, or falls. Monofilament exam reveals decreased sensation in bilateral great toes and metatarsal heads. Deep tendon reflexes reduced at ankles. Discussed possibility of diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Ordered repeat HbA1c and nerve conduction studies (NCS) to evaluate neuropathy. Referral to endocrinology considered if glycemic control remains suboptimal.

chief_complaint.value: Lab review, persistent neuropathy symptoms, endocrinology referral

notes.value: Patient returns for lab review and discussion of neuropathy symptoms. Repeat HbA1c is 8.0%, showing only minimal improvement. Neuropathy symptoms persist, affecting quality of life. Nerve conduction studies (NCS) results indicate mild-moderate sensorimotor polyneuropathy, consistent with diabetic neuropathy. Given persistent hyperglycemia and confirmed neuropathy despite maximal oral therapy, decided to refer to Dr. Alex Lee for endocrinology consultation to optimize diabetes management, likely involving insulin initiation. Provided patient with referral details and explained the rationale for starting insulin.

chief_complaint.value: Endocrinology consult for Type 2 Diabetes and neuropathy

notes.value: New patient endocrinology consult for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus with recent onset diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Reviewed PCP notes, current medications, and lab results (HbA1c 8.0%). Patient reports foot numbness and tingling, worse at night. Confirmed diagnosis of diabetic peripheral neuropathy based on symptoms and NCS. Discussed treatment intensification including insulin therapy. Started Insulin Glargine 20 units daily at bedtime. Provided detailed education on insulin administration, glucose monitoring, and hypoglycemia prevention. Emphasized lifestyle modifications. Patient will monitor blood glucose daily and adjust insulin with guidance. Follow-up in 6 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Follow-up for insulin titration and neuropathy symptoms

notes.value: Patient returns for follow-up after 6 weeks on Insulin Glargine. Reports improved fasting blood sugars, now consistently in 100-130 mg/dL range. Postprandial sugars also showing improvement. Neuropathy symptoms of numbness/tingling are stable, not worsening, but not significantly improved either. Discussed blood glucose logs and adjusted insulin glargine dose to 24 units daily. Emphasized continued foot care and regular glucose monitoring. Ordered repeat HbA1c and lipid panel for next visit. Encouraged referral to podiatry if symptoms progress. Scheduled next follow-up in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: Routine check-up, care coordination with endocrinology

notes.value: Patient presents for routine PCP check-up. Reports overall improved energy levels and less thirst since starting insulin. Glycemic control appears better based on home monitoring. Neuropathy symptoms remain consistent, no acute exacerbation. BP 130/80. Confirmed adherence to all medications. Reviewed recent communication from Dr. Lee confirming stable diabetes management. Discussed fall prevention strategies given peripheral neuropathy. Emphasized continued healthy diet and exercise. Next PCP visit in 6 months for general health maintenance and preventative screenings.

Patient returns for quarterly endocrinology follow-up. Reports good glycemic control with stable blood glucose levels. HbA1c 6.8% (at target). Neuropathy symptoms are stable, no significant improvement but also no worsening. Continues to use Insulin Glargine 24 units daily. Discussed importance of regular foot exams. Ordered annual microalbuminuria screening which showed trace albumin. Reinforced lifestyle modifications to protect kidney function. Reviewed medications, all remain active. Next follow-up in 3 months.

summary.value: 53-year-old female diagnosed with hypothyroidism and hyperlipidemia, currently managed with levothyroxine and atorvastatin with good clinical control.

notes.value:
Patient presents with a 3-month history of increasing fatigue, unintentional weight gain of 8 lbs, and general feeling of sluggishness. Denies fever, chills, night sweats. Reports mild constipation and dry skin. On review of systems, no other significant complaints. No known chronic medical conditions or regular medications. Family history significant for hypothyroidism in mother.

Physical exam reveals mild bradycardia (HR 58 bpm), normal BP. Skin appears dry. No goiter palpable. Neurological exam normal. Ordered TSH, CBC, CMP, and Vitamin D levels to investigate symptoms. Will follow up with lab results.

chief_complaint.value: Lab review, worsening fatigue and cold intolerance

notes.value:
Patient returns for lab review. TSH returned significantly elevated at 8.9 mIU/L (normal range 0.4-4.0). CBC, CMP, and Vitamin D levels were all within normal limits. Patient reports worsening fatigue and new onset of cold intolerance. These symptoms, coupled with elevated TSH, are highly suggestive of primary hypothyroidism.

Discussed diagnosis and initiated Levothyroxine 50mcg daily. Counseled on medication administration (take on empty stomach, separate from other medications/supplements by 4 hours). Emphasized importance of adherence and follow-up for dose titration. Will recheck TSH in 6 weeks to assess response.

chief_complaint.value: Follow-up on Levothyroxine, new lipid panel results

notes.value:
Patient reports significant improvement in fatigue, cold intolerance, and energy levels since starting Levothyroxine. Weight is stable. Overall feeling much better. A fasting lipid panel was ordered at the last visit to screen for hyperlipidemia given family history (father with hyperlipidemia).

Lipid panel results show Total Cholesterol 245 mg/dL (high), LDL 160 mg/dL (high), HDL 55 mg/dL (normal), Triglycerides 130 mg/dL (normal). Based on these results, patient is diagnosed with hyperlipidemia. Discussed lifestyle modifications including diet and exercise. Initiated Atorvastatin 20mg daily for hyperlipidemia management. Counseled on potential side effects and importance of adherence. Will recheck TSH and lipid panel in 3 months.

chief_complaint.value: Check TSH and lipid levels, medication review

notes.value:
Patient returns for follow-up on hypothyroidism and hyperlipidemia management. Reports continued improvement in hypothyroid symptoms. No muscle aches or other side effects from atorvastatin. Labs from today show TSH at 2.8 mIU/L (well-controlled). Lipid panel shows Total Cholesterol 210 mg/dL, LDL 125 mg/dL, HDL 58 mg/dL. While improved, LDL is still above target.

Increased Atorvastatin dosage to 40mg daily to achieve better LDL control. Continued Levothyroxine 50mcg daily. Reinforced diet and exercise recommendations. Scheduled next follow-up in 4 months to re-evaluate lipid profile and TSH.

Patient presents for routine follow-up. Reports feeling well, with no recurrence of hypothyroid symptoms or new concerns related to hyperlipidemia. No side effects from medications. Adherent to both Levothyroxine and Atorvastatin 40mg daily. Continues with regular walking and a balanced diet.

Physical exam stable. Blood pressure 125/78 mmHg. Heart rate 62 bpm. No new findings. Will defer labs to the annual physical next year, given good clinical control and patient stability. Encouraged continued lifestyle measures and medication adherence. Next appointment scheduled for annual physical.

Annual physical examination. Patient reports sustained excellent control of her hypothyroidism and hyperlipidemia. No complaints of fatigue, weight fluctuations, or muscle pain. Continues to take both medications as prescribed. Adherence is excellent. Reviewed diet and exercise habits, which remain consistent.

Labs today show TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal), Total Cholesterol 185 mg/dL (normal), LDL 92 mg/dL (normal), HDL 60 mg/dL (normal). All parameters are now within target ranges. Will continue Levothyroxine 50mcg and Atorvastatin 40mg daily. Encouraged continued regular physical activity and healthy eating. Next annual physical in one year.

summary.value: 37-year-old male presenting with seasonal allergies and intermittent wheezing, diagnosed with Allergic Rhinitis and later with Moderate Persistent Asthma, managed with inhaled corticosteroids and rescue inhaler.

notes.value:
Mr. Brown, a 37-year-old male, presents with symptoms consistent with seasonal allergies, including sneezing, runny nose, and itchy eyes, worse during spring. He also reports intermittent wheezing, particularly after exercise or exposure to cold air, but denies significant shortness of breath or nocturnal awakenings. History reveals similar symptoms since childhood, but never formally diagnosed with asthma. No known food or drug allergies.

Physical exam reveals clear nasal discharge and mild conjunctival injection. Lungs clear to auscultation at rest, but mild expiratory wheezes noted after a brief stair climb. Diagnosed with Allergic Rhinitis. Prescribed Loratadine 10mg daily as needed for allergy symptoms. Ordered baseline Pulmonary Function Tests (PFTs) to evaluate lung function and rule out underlying asthma. Follow-up in 2 weeks for PFT review.

Mr. Brown returns for review of PFT results. Spirometry shows FEV1 78% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.68, consistent with mild obstructive pattern that improves by 15% post-bronchodilator, strongly suggestive of asthma. He reports that while Loratadine has helped with allergy symptoms, his intermittent wheezing persists, especially with physical activity.

Given the PFT findings and persistent symptoms, a diagnosis of suspected asthma is made. Due to the chronic nature and obstructive pattern, a referral to Allergy & Immunology is initiated for further evaluation, confirmation, and management of asthma. Patient advised to avoid known triggers and continue Loratadine. Referral letter prepared and sent.

Mr. Brown presents for an Allergy & Immunology consultation following referral from his PCP for suspected asthma. Reviewed PCP notes and PFTs. Patient's symptoms (intermittent wheezing, exercise-induced, seasonal allergy history) are highly consistent with asthma. Further skin prick testing confirms sensitivity to common environmental allergens (dust mites, tree pollen).

Diagnosis of Asthma, Moderate Persistent, confirmed. Initiated Albuterol HFA 90mcg/puff, 2 puffs as needed for wheezing or before exercise, and Fluticasone Propionate (Flovent HFA) 110mcg, 2 puffs twice daily as a maintenance inhaled corticosteroid. Reviewed inhaler technique in detail. Advised on trigger avoidance and the importance of daily maintenance therapy. Follow-up in 4 weeks to assess asthma control.

outcome.value: Positive for dust mites, tree pollen

notes.value:
Mr. Brown returns for follow-up regarding his asthma management. He reports significant improvement in symptoms since starting Fluticasone daily. Wheezing episodes are less frequent and less severe, primarily occurring only with intense exercise or significant allergen exposure. He uses his Albuterol rescue inhaler 1-2 times per week. No nocturnal awakenings due to asthma. He feels his breathing is much better overall.

Physical exam reveals clear lung sounds bilaterally without wheezes. Peak expiratory flow (PEF) measurements are consistently in his green zone. Reviewed proper inhaler technique and adherence to medication regimen. Asthma Action Plan reviewed and provided. Continue current medications. Follow-up in 3 months or as needed for worsening symptoms.

Mr. Brown presents for a routine primary care follow-up. He reports his asthma has been well-controlled since his last visit with Dr. Lee. No recent exacerbations or emergency room visits. He continues to take Fluticasone daily and uses Albuterol rarely. His seasonal allergy symptoms are mild with Loratadine.

Discussed fall allergy season preparation and importance of flu shot. Reviewed medication adherence. Lung exam clear. Overall, doing well. Encouraged continued adherence to his asthma action plan and regular follow-ups with his allergist. Referred for routine annual physical in 6 months.

Mr. Brown is here for his quarterly asthma review. He states his asthma remains well-controlled. He's had no significant flares or increase in rescue inhaler use, even during the change of seasons. He continues on Fluticasone daily and uses Albuterol only as needed, approximately once a month.

Objective measures including PEF readings are stable. Allergy symptoms are minimal. Emphasized the importance of annual influenza vaccination and discussed pneumococcal vaccine readiness given his chronic condition. Continue current medication regimen. Next follow-up scheduled for 6 months, or sooner if symptoms worsen.

summary.value: 60-year-old female with a history of progressive polyarthritis, initially managed as osteoarthritis, later diagnosed with seropositive Rheumatoid Arthritis, now also diagnosed with osteoporosis following a fragility fracture.

condition_072.label: Osteoporosis with current pathological fracture, unspecified site

name.value: Osteoporosis with current pathological fracture, unspecified site

chief_complaint.value: Bilateral knee pain, worsening over 6 months

notes.value:
Patient is a 60-year-old female presenting with bilateral knee pain, gradually worsening over the past 6 months. Pain is achy, worse with activity and relieved by rest. Reports occasional stiffness in the morning lasting about 15-20 minutes. No swelling or redness noted. Denies fever or systemic symptoms. Patient attributes pain to 'old age'.

Physical exam reveals mild crepitus in both knees, with full range of motion but pain on extreme flexion. No warmth or effusion. Initial diagnosis of osteoarthritis. Started ibuprofen 400mg PRN. Recommended physical therapy referral and follow-up in 4 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent knee pain, new hand and wrist stiffness

notes.value:
Patient returns reporting persistent knee pain despite ibuprofen and physical therapy. Now also experiencing new onset stiffness and pain in bilateral hands and wrists, particularly in the mornings, lasting over an hour. Reports general fatigue. No fever or weight changes. Family history notable for mother with RA.

Physical exam reveals mild tenderness and swelling in bilateral MCPs and wrists. Knees show continued crepitus. Given polyarticular symptoms, prolonged morning stiffness, and family history, concern for inflammatory arthritis. Ordered CBC, ESR, CRP, Rheumatoid Factor (RF), and anti-CCP antibodies. Referral to Rheumatology initiated.

New patient consultation for polyarticular joint pain. Reviewed PCP notes and labs: ESR 35, CRP 12.5. Additional labs from today show RF positive (150 IU/mL, ref <14), anti-CCP antibodies positive (120 U/mL, ref <20). Patient meets ACR/EULAR criteria for Rheumatoid Arthritis.

Diagnosis: Seropositive Rheumatoid Arthritis. Discussed treatment options. Initiated Methotrexate 15mg weekly, with Folic Acid 1mg daily. Also started Prednisone 10mg daily as bridging therapy, with a plan to taper off gradually as Methotrexate takes effect. Baseline labs for Methotrexate monitoring (CBC, LFTs) ordered. Follow-up in 4 weeks.

Patient returns for follow-up on Methotrexate and Prednisone. Reports significant improvement in joint pain and stiffness, morning stiffness now lasting less than 30 minutes. Fatigue also improved. Tolerating Methotrexate well, no significant side effects reported. Prednisone taper initiated, currently at 7.5mg daily.

Labs show stable CBC and LFTs, confirming good tolerance to Methotrexate. ESR and CRP trending down. Ibuprofen discontinued as symptoms are better controlled with current regimen. Advised to continue current medications and taper prednisone as planned. Next visit in 6 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Acute back pain after minor fall

notes.value:
Patient presented to urgent care with acute onset severe lower back pain after a minor fall at home (tripped on rug). Pain is localized to the thoracic-lumbar junction, 8/10, exacerbated by movement. No neurological deficits noted. Currently still on prednisone 2.5mg daily, planning to stop within the week.

Physical exam reveals significant tenderness over T12-L1 vertebrae. X-ray of thoracic and lumbar spine performed, revealing a new T12 compression fracture. Given patient's age and minor trauma, concern for underlying osteoporosis. Prednisone discontinued. Ordered DEXA scan and referral to Endocrinology. Discharged with pain medication and strict instructions for back precautions.

outcome.value: New T12 compression fracture identified.

notes.value:
New patient endocrinology consultation for osteoporosis following a T12 compression fracture. Patient recently completed a prednisone taper for RA. DEXA scan results reviewed: T-score -2.8 at lumbar spine, -2.6 at femoral neck. Confirmed diagnosis of Osteoporosis.

Discussed bone health management. Initiated Alendronate 70mg weekly. Recommended Calcium 1200mg daily and Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily. Emphasized importance of fall prevention and good nutrition. Baseline labs for calcium and vitamin D ordered. Follow-up in 3 months to monitor treatment response.

outcome.value: Osteoporosis confirmed (Lumbar spine T-score -2.8, Femoral neck T-score -2.6).

chief_complaint.value: Quarterly Rheumatology follow-up, RA and osteoporosis

notes.value:
Routine quarterly follow-up for Rheumatoid Arthritis. Patient reports good control of RA symptoms on Methotrexate and Folic Acid. No new flares. Joint stiffness minimal, fatigue well-managed. Also reports starting Alendronate for osteoporosis and tolerating it well. Back pain from fracture has largely resolved.

Exam shows no active synovitis. Review of labs indicates continued stable CBC and LFTs, ESR 15, CRP 4.8. Discussed coordination of care with Dr. Sokolov for osteoporosis management. Continue Methotrexate, Folic Acid. Next rheumatology visit in 3 months. Encourage continued adherence to Alendronate and Calcium/Vitamin D.

summary.value: 22-year-old male presenting with acute cough and congestion, diagnosed with acute bronchitis, and managed symptomatically with full resolution.

notes.value:
Patient is a 22-year-old male presenting with a 5-day history of productive cough, sore throat, and nasal congestion. Reports occasional low-grade fever (max 100.5°F) and mild body aches. Denies shortness of breath, chest pain, or significant wheezing. No known sick contacts. Vaccinated for influenza. 

On exam, afebrile, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Throat mildly erythematous with no exudates. Nasal mucosa congested. Diagnosed with acute bronchitis, likely viral. Advised symptomatic management with OTC medications. Prescribed Ibuprofen for fever/pain and Dextromethorphan/Guaifenesin for cough. Advised to return if symptoms worsen or new symptoms develop.

Patient returns for follow-up regarding acute bronchitis. Reports significant improvement in cough, now only occasional and non-productive. Sore throat and congestion have resolved. No fever or body aches for the past 3 days. Feels much better and is returning to normal activities. 

On exam, lungs remain clear. No fever. Patient confirms adherence to symptomatic treatment. Given resolution of symptoms, advised to discontinue OTC medications as needed. Patient educated on prevention strategies for future respiratory infections. Condition resolved.

summary.value: 31-year-old female presenting with dysuria and frequency, diagnosed with an uncomplicated urinary tract infection, successfully treated with a short course of antibiotics.

notes.value: Patient presents with 2 days of dysuria, urinary frequency, and urgency. Denies fever, chills, flank pain, or vaginal discharge. No recent sexual activity. Physical exam unremarkable. Urinalysis ordered, and empiric Ciprofloxacin prescribed pending culture results. Patient advised to return for follow-up to discuss results and symptom resolution.

value.value: Leukocyte Esterase positive, Nitrites positive, WBC 20-30/hpf

chief_complaint.value: Follow-up on UTI symptoms and culture results

notes.value: Patient reports complete resolution of dysuria, frequency, and urgency after 5 days of Ciprofloxacin. Urine culture results from previous visit grew E. coli (>100,000 CFU/mL) sensitive to Ciprofloxacin, confirming the diagnosis. Patient completed the 7-day course of antibiotics without side effects. Advised on proper hygiene and adequate hydration to prevent recurrence. No further follow-up needed unless symptoms recur.

summary.value: 45-year-old male presenting with acute low back pain after heavy lifting, managed conservatively with NSAIDs and physical therapy, resulting in complete resolution.

chief_complaint.value: Acute low back pain after lifting

notes.value: Patient presents with sudden onset low back pain after lifting a heavy box yesterday. Pain is 8/10, localized to lumbar region, non-radiating. No neurological deficits, no bowel/bladder changes. Physical exam shows tenderness to palpation in lumbar paraspinal muscles, limited flexion. Straight leg raise negative. Impression: Acute lumbar strain. Prescribed Ibuprofen 600mg TID. Advised rest, heat/ice, and gentle stretching. Return in 1 week if no improvement.

Patient returns reporting significant improvement in low back pain. Pain is now 2/10, occurring only with specific movements. Has been compliant with Ibuprofen and self-care measures. Physical exam shows mild residual tenderness, full range of motion without pain. Patient feels much better and able to return to normal activities. Impression: Resolved acute lumbar strain. Advised to continue gentle activity, discontinue Ibuprofen as pain resolves. No further follow-up needed unless symptoms recur.

summary.value: 28-year-old female presenting with an acute pruritic erythematous rash on her arms and chest, diagnosed as contact dermatitis likely due to a new laundry detergent, successfully treated with topical corticosteroids.

chief_complaint.value: New, itchy rash on arms and chest

notes.value: Patient presents with a 3-day history of an intensely pruritic, erythematous, mildly papular rash affecting both forearms and spreading to the upper chest. Reports changing to a new 'eco-friendly' laundry detergent approximately one week prior. Denies fever, chills, systemic symptoms. No known food allergies or new medications. Physical exam reveals well-demarcated patches of erythema with some papules and excoriations on bilateral extensor surfaces of forearms and upper sternal area. Impression: Acute Contact Dermatitis. Advised to discontinue new detergent and switch back to previous one. Prescribed Hydrocortisone Cream 1% twice daily. Return in 2 weeks for follow-up.

Patient returns for follow-up regarding contact dermatitis. Reports significant improvement in rash and itching. Switched back to old laundry detergent immediately after last visit. States rash is almost entirely resolved, with only faint residual erythema and no active pruritus. Has been using hydrocortisone cream as directed. Physical exam shows clear skin with no active lesions, only mild post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. Advised to continue avoiding the offending detergent. Discontinue hydrocortisone cream as rash resolved. Patient is pleased with outcome. No further follow-up needed unless recurrence.

summary.value: 56-year-old male presenting with sudden onset positional vertigo, diagnosed with Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV) and successfully treated with the Epley maneuver.

chief_complaint.value: Sudden onset severe dizziness with head movement

notes.value:
Patient is a 56-year-old male presenting with acute onset vertigo, experiencing severe spinning sensations, nausea, and imbalance when changing head position, especially lying down or turning in bed. Symptoms started 2 days ago. Denies hearing loss, tinnitus, headache, or focal neurological deficits. No recent head trauma or illness.

Physical exam notable for normal otoscopy, no nystagmus at rest. Dix-Hallpike maneuver performed, eliciting classic torsional nystagmus with latency, fatigability, and symptoms, consistent with right posterior canal BPPV. Given clear clinical picture, referred to Otolaryngology for definitive treatment. Prescribed Meclizine for symptomatic relief.

chief_complaint.value: Otolaryngology consult for vertigo, BPPV treatment

notes.value:
Patient presented to Otolaryngology following referral for BPPV. Confirmed history of positional vertigo consistent with prior PCP assessment. Repeat Dix-Hallpike maneuver performed, confirming right posterior canal BPPV with characteristic nystagmus and vertigo.

Epley maneuver (canalith repositioning procedure) was performed successfully. Post-procedure, repeat Dix-Hallpike maneuver was negative, with no nystagmus or reported vertigo. Patient tolerated the procedure well and reported significant improvement in symptoms. Advised to avoid provocative head movements for 24-48 hours and to sleep propped up. Discontinued Meclizine as symptoms resolved. Patient to follow up if symptoms recur, otherwise no further ENT follow-up needed.

outcome.value: Successful, complete resolution of positional vertigo

summary.value: 35-year-old female presenting with acute onset of facial pain and nasal congestion, diagnosed with acute sinusitis, managed with symptomatic treatment.

chief_complaint.value: Acute onset of facial pain and nasal congestion

notes.value:
Patient presents with a 3-day history of facial pressure, especially around the cheeks and forehead, accompanied by thick, yellowish nasal discharge and a dull headache. Denies fever, chills, or vision changes. Has tried OTC decongestants with minimal relief. Past medical history otherwise unremarkable.

Physical exam reveals mild erythema and swelling of nasal mucosa, with tenderness to palpation over the maxillary sinuses. Oropharyngeal exam is benign. No lymphadenopathy. Lungs clear to auscultation. Clinical diagnosis of acute sinusitis. Advised symptomatic management including saline nasal rinses, hydration, and initiated Ibuprofen for pain and inflammation. Counseled on signs of worsening symptoms requiring re-evaluation.

Patient returns for follow-up regarding acute sinusitis symptoms. Reports significant improvement since the last visit. Nasal congestion has cleared, facial pain is completely resolved, and discharge has ceased. States she is feeling much better and has discontinued Ibuprofen as needed for the past 2 days. No new complaints.

Physical exam today is unremarkable. Nasal passages are clear, no tenderness over sinuses. Oropharynx clear. Lungs clear. Confirmed resolution of acute sinusitis. Reassured patient about the self-limiting nature of viral sinusitis and advised to continue good hydration and rest. Ibuprofen is officially discontinued. Patient is well and no further follow-up is immediately required.

summary.value: 42-year-old male presenting with right elbow pain consistent with lateral epicondylitis, managed initially with NSAIDs and subsequently resolved after physical therapy.

notes.value:
Patient presents with persistent right elbow pain for the past two months, insidious onset, aggravated by activities involving wrist extension and gripping, such as using tools or lifting groceries. Pain is localized to the lateral aspect of the elbow. Denies trauma, numbness, or tingling. Work involves some manual labor.

Physical exam reveals tenderness over the lateral epicondyle, positive Cozen's test and Maudsley's test on the right. Range of motion of elbow and wrist is full and pain-free passively. No swelling or erythema. Assessment: Lateral Epicondylitis (Tennis Elbow), right. Prescribed Ibuprofen for pain relief. Advised activity modification and stretching. Follow up in 2 weeks.

Patient returns for follow-up regarding right elbow pain. Reports slight improvement with Ibuprofen but pain remains bothersome, especially with prolonged activity. Continues to have difficulty with gripping and lifting.

Physical exam findings are similar to the previous visit, with persistent tenderness over the lateral epicondyle. Discussed options including further conservative management. Given limited improvement with NSAIDs and activity modification, recommended a course of physical therapy. Referral placed to Dr. Michael Lee for evaluation and treatment. Will follow up after PT completion.

chief_complaint.value: Improved elbow pain after physical therapy

notes.value:
Patient presents for follow-up after completing a 6-week course of physical therapy for right lateral epicondylitis. Reports significant improvement in elbow pain, now rated 1/10 (down from 7/10 at peak). Able to perform daily activities, including work tasks, with minimal discomfort. Denies any residual weakness or functional limitation.

Physical exam today shows no tenderness over the lateral epicondyle. Cozen's and Maudsley's tests are negative. Full, pain-free range of motion. Patient is very satisfied with the outcome of physical therapy. Ibuprofen discontinued. Advised to continue home exercise program and return if symptoms recur. Condition considered resolved.

summary.value: 25-year-old female presenting with acute onset of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, diagnosed with acute gastroenteritis and managed with symptomatic care and hydration.

chief_complaint.value: Acute onset of severe nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea

notes.value:
Patient presents to clinic reporting sudden onset of severe nausea, multiple episodes of non-bloody vomiting, and watery diarrhea starting approximately 12 hours ago. Denies fever, chills, or recent travel. Reports decreased oral intake due to nausea. Physical examination reveals dry mucous membranes and mild orthostatic changes, indicative of mild dehydration. Abdomen is soft, non-tender, with hyperactive bowel sounds. Diagnosed with acute gastroenteritis.

Advised on oral rehydration therapy. Prescribed ondansetron 4mg orally as needed for nausea. Labs ordered to assess hydration status and rule out electrolyte abnormalities. Follow-up scheduled in 3 days for symptom re-evaluation and lab review.

Patient returns for follow-up regarding acute gastroenteritis symptoms. Reports significant improvement: no vomiting or diarrhea in the last 24 hours, nausea has resolved. Tolerating oral fluids and a bland diet well. States she used ondansetron twice on the first day, but no longer needs it. Physical exam shows improved hydration, moist mucous membranes, and stable vital signs. Abdomen remains soft, non-tender, with normoactive bowel sounds.

Lab results reviewed: Sodium and Potassium have normalized, Creatinine also returned to baseline. Advised to gradually reintroduce normal diet, continue good hand hygiene. Ondansetron discontinued as symptoms have fully resolved. Patient educated on potential for recurrence and when to seek medical attention.

summary.value: 67-year-old male presenting with a raised, waxy lesion on his back, diagnosed as seborrheic keratosis, managed with observation and reassurance.

notes.value:
Patient presents with a raised, dark brown, waxy lesion on his upper back, noticed approximately 6 months ago. Asymptomatic but patient concerned about appearance and potential malignancy. Denies pain, itching, or bleeding. History of extensive sun exposure in youth. No other concerning skin lesions or systemic symptoms.

Physical exam revealed a well-circumscribed, verrucous, pigmented lesion approximately 1.5 cm in diameter on the right scapular region, consistent with seborrheic keratosis. No features suggestive of melanoma or squamous cell carcinoma. Advised reassurance regarding benign nature. Started daily low-dose Aspirin for primary cardiovascular prevention and emollient cream for any mild irritation.

chief_complaint.value: Routine check-up, follow-up on back lesion

notes.value:
Patient returns for routine check-up and follow-up on the seborrheic keratosis. Reports no changes in the lesion; it remains asymptomatic. Patient is reassured by the benign diagnosis provided previously. No new skin lesions or other dermatological concerns. General health good, tolerating Aspirin and emollient cream well.

Physical exam confirmed the seborrheic keratosis on the upper back appears stable, with no concerning changes in size, color, or texture. No new suspicious lesions noted. Reassured patient again. Advised continued observation and to report any changes. Routine labs for general health ordered.

summary.value: 39-year-old female presenting with progressive right hand and wrist pain, numbness, and tingling, ultimately diagnosed with Carpal Tunnel Syndrome confirmed by nerve conduction study, and managed with splinting and anti-inflammatory medication.

notes.value: Patient reports gradual onset of numbness and tingling in her right thumb, index, middle, and half of the ring finger over the past 2 months. Symptoms are worse at night and with repetitive hand movements (typing, driving). Reports occasional shooting pain into the forearm. Denies trauma or recent injury. Exam reveals positive Phalen's and Tinel's signs at the right wrist. Motor strength intact. Provisional diagnosis of Carpal Tunnel Syndrome. Prescribed Naproxen for pain and inflammation, and recommended a neutral wrist splint for night use. Ordered nerve conduction study to confirm diagnosis. Follow up in 2 weeks.

Patient returns for review of nerve conduction study (NCS) results. Reports slight improvement in symptoms with Naproxen and consistent night splint use, but still experiences intermittent numbness. NCS confirmed mild to moderate right median nerve compression at the wrist. Given the clinical picture and supportive NCS, diagnosis of Carpal Tunnel Syndrome of the right hand is established. Continued conservative management with splinting and Naproxen. Discussed ergonomic modifications at work. Will follow up in 1 month to assess progress.

procedure_049.label: Nerve Conduction Study - Right Upper Extremity

name.value: Nerve Conduction Study - Right Upper Extremity

outcome.value: Confirmed mild to moderate median nerve compression at wrist

notes.value: Patient reports significant improvement in symptoms since last visit. Numbness and tingling are now infrequent and milder, primarily occurring with prolonged computer use. Night symptoms have resolved with continuous splinting. Able to perform daily activities with much less discomfort. Advised to continue night splinting and ergonomic modifications. Naproxen may be tapered and discontinued if symptoms remain controlled. Patient is satisfied with conservative management. No surgical intervention currently indicated. Follow up as needed.

summary.value: 50-year-old male presenting with acute onset of right eye redness, discharge, and irritation, diagnosed with bacterial conjunctivitis and successfully treated with topical antibiotic eye drops.

notes.value: Patient presents with acute onset of right eye redness, mild irritation, and purulent discharge for the past 24 hours. Denies pain, vision changes, or foreign body sensation. Left eye is clear. No fever or recent upper respiratory infection. On exam, right conjunctiva is injected, purulent discharge noted, eyelids mildly swollen. Preauricular lymph nodes non-tender. Visual acuity 20/20 in both eyes. Impression: Acute Bacterial Conjunctivitis, right eye. Prescribed Tobramycin Ophthalmic Solution. Advised strict hand hygiene and to avoid touching the eye. Follow up in 3-5 days or sooner if worsening.

Patient returns for follow-up regarding right eye conjunctivitis. Reports significant improvement of symptoms. Redness and discharge have resolved almost completely. No irritation. Exam shows minimal conjunctival injection, no discharge. Eyelids normal. Visual acuity unchanged. Patient completed 7 days of Tobramycin as prescribed. Condition resolved. Advised to discontinue eye drops. Patient instructed to return if symptoms recur or worsen.

summary.value: 33-year-old female presenting with chronic right heel pain, diagnosed with Plantar Fasciitis, managed with conservative measures including stretching and activity modification.

notes.value:
Patient presents with a 2-month history of right heel pain, which is most severe with the first steps in the morning and after periods of rest. She reports the pain improves somewhat with activity but worsens again after prolonged standing or walking. Denies trauma, numbness, or tingling. She has tried OTC pain relievers with minimal benefit.

Physical examination revealed tenderness to palpation at the medial calcaneal tubercle of the right foot. Dorsiflexion of the ankle with toe extension elicited pain. No swelling or erythema noted. Strength and sensation intact. Clinical diagnosis of Plantar Fasciitis made. Patient was advised on conservative management including calf and plantar fascia stretching exercises, ice application, supportive footwear, and activity modification. Recommended OTC Ibuprofen for pain.

chief_complaint.value: Follow-up for persistent right heel pain

notes.value:
Patient returns for follow-up regarding her right heel pain. She reports a slight improvement in the severity and duration of morning stiffness since initiating the stretching exercises and ensuring supportive footwear. Pain is still present, especially towards the end of the day or after significant walking. She is using Ibuprofen occasionally, which provides some relief.

Re-emphasized the importance of consistent stretching, proper footwear, and avoiding aggravating activities. Reviewed correct stretching techniques. Discussed the chronic nature of Plantar Fasciitis and advised to continue conservative management for at least another 4-6 weeks. Offered referral to physical therapy if symptoms do not continue to improve. Patient opted to continue home exercises for now. Return as needed.

summary.value: 58-year-old male presenting with progressive fatigue and weakness, diagnosed with Vitamin B12 Deficiency after lab confirmation, and initiated on oral supplementation.

chief_complaint.value: Persistent fatigue and weakness for 3 months

notes.value:
Patient is a 58-year-old male presenting with a 3-month history of progressive fatigue, generalized weakness, and occasional tingling sensation in both feet. Symptoms are gradually worsening, affecting daily activities. Denies fever, chills, weight loss, or changes in bowel habits. Diet includes occasional meat and dairy.

Physical exam is largely unremarkable. Neurological exam reveals intact sensation to light touch but decreased vibratory sense at the ankles bilaterally. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ symmetrical. Based on history, suspected Vitamin B12 deficiency. Ordered labs including CBC, CMP, and Vitamin B12 level. Started empirical Vitamin B12 supplementation 1000mcg daily pending lab results. Advised to return for lab review in 2 weeks.

chief_complaint.value: Lab review for fatigue, follow-up on B12 supplementation

notes.value:
Patient returns for lab review. Reports mild improvement in energy levels and a slight decrease in the tingling sensation in his feet since starting Vitamin B12 supplementation. Lab results confirm Vitamin B12 deficiency: level of 180 pg/mL. CBC is within normal limits. Other labs unremarkable.

Diagnosis of Vitamin B12 Deficiency confirmed. Discussed the importance of continued daily Vitamin B12 supplementation. Explained potential causes, including dietary intake and malabsorption. Will continue current oral supplementation dosage. Scheduled follow-up in 3 months to recheck Vitamin B12 levels and assess symptom resolution.

summary.value: 26-year-old female presenting with acute sore throat and low-grade fever, diagnosed with acute pharyngitis after negative strep test.

chief_complaint.value: Sore throat and low-grade fever for 2 days

notes.value:
Patient presents with chief complaint of sudden onset sore throat, described as scratchy and painful, worsening with swallowing. Reports associated low-grade fever (max 100.5F at home), mild fatigue, and body aches. Denies cough, runny nose, or difficulty breathing. No known sick contacts. Last oral intake was a small amount of soup due to discomfort.

Physical exam reveals erythematous pharynx with mild tonsillar exudates. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Lungs clear to auscultation. Ordered rapid strep test to rule out streptococcal pharyngitis. Advised symptomatic care with acetaminophen and throat lozenges. Will follow up with lab results.

chief_complaint.value: Strep test results review, improving symptoms

notes.value:
Patient returns for review of rapid strep test results and follow-up on symptoms. Rapid strep test was negative. Patient reports significant improvement in sore throat and fever since starting acetaminophen and using throat lozenges. Fever resolved, throat pain is now mild and intermittent. Fatigue has lessened. Able to swallow solids with minimal discomfort.

Physical exam today shows resolving pharyngeal erythema. No exudates. Patient reassured that this is likely a viral pharyngitis. Advised to continue symptomatic care as needed and to complete rest. Medications (Acetaminophen, Throat Lozenges) discontinued as symptoms are largely resolved. Return if symptoms worsen or new symptoms develop.

summary.value: 73-year-old male presenting with a painful vesicular rash in a dermatomal distribution, diagnosed with Herpes Zoster and treated with oral valacyclovir.

chief_complaint.value: Painful rash on right side of chest

notes.value:
Mr. Chen, a 73-year-old male, presents with a painful rash on his right anterior chest wall for 2 days. He describes it as burning and tingling, followed by the eruption of small blisters. The rash follows a clear dermatomal pattern (T4-T5). Denies recent trauma or known exposure to allergens. Immunization history: unsure about shingles vaccine.

Physical exam reveals multiple erythematous papules and vesicles within a C-shaped band on the right chest, consistent with a T4-T5 dermatome. Lesions are tender to palpation. No signs of secondary infection. Based on clinical presentation, a diagnosis of Herpes Zoster (Shingles) is made. Initiated Valacyclovir 1000mg three times daily for 7 days. Also prescribed Acetaminophen 500mg PRN for pain. Patient counseled on pain management, keeping the rash clean and dry, and the importance of antiviral adherence. Follow-up in 1 week to assess progress.

Patient returns for follow-up on his shingles. Reports significant improvement in pain, now rating it 2/10, compared to 7/10 at initial presentation. Rash is crusting over and vesicles have resolved. No new lesions. Completed the 7-day course of Valacyclovir yesterday. Still using Acetaminophen occasionally for residual discomfort, but less frequently.

Physical exam shows healing, crusted lesions along the T4-T5 dermatome on the right chest. No active vesiculation. No signs of infection. Patient tolerated antiviral medication well. Discussed potential for post-herpetic neuralgia and advised to continue symptomatic pain management as needed. Scheduled a routine follow-up in 3 weeks to ensure complete resolution and assess for any long-term pain.

Patient presents for routine follow-up regarding his recent Herpes Zoster episode. Reports complete resolution of the rash. No residual pain or paresthesias, indicating no significant post-herpetic neuralgia. Has not needed Acetaminophen for the past week. Feeling well and back to usual activities.

Physical exam reveals faint post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation along the T4-T5 dermatome where the rash was, but skin integrity is intact. No tenderness or sensory deficits. Reassured patient about the excellent recovery. Discussed shingles vaccination for future prevention. Encourage yearly flu shot and general health maintenance. Patient advised to return for any new concerns.

description.value: Chronic autoimmune disease affecting multiple organ systems including skin, joints, kidneys, brain, and blood cells. Characterized by periods of flares and remission with autoantibody-mediated tissue damage.

symptoms.value: Malar rash, joint pain, fatigue, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, serositis, hair loss, Raynaud phenomenon, fever, weight loss

diagnostic_criteria.value: ACR/EULAR 2019 classification criteria requiring ANA positivity plus weighted score of clinical and immunological domains. Key domains include constitutional symptoms, hematologic abnormalities, neuropsychiatric involvement, mucocutaneous findings, serosal inflammation, musculoskeletal disease, and renal involvement.

lab_markers.value: ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith antibody, low complement C3/C4, cytopenias (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia), elevated ESR, positive lupus band test on skin biopsy

epidemiology.value: Prevalence 20-150 per 100,000 population. Female-to-male ratio approximately 9:1. Higher prevalence in African American, Hispanic, and Asian populations. Typical onset between ages 15-45.

description.value: Chronic inflammatory autoimmune disorder primarily targeting synovial joints, causing symmetric polyarthritis with potential for joint destruction and systemic manifestations.

symptoms.value: Morning stiffness lasting over 30 minutes, symmetric joint swelling, joint pain, fatigue, rheumatoid nodules, low-grade fever, weight loss

diagnostic_criteria.value: 2010 ACR/EULAR classification criteria: joint involvement pattern, serology (RF, anti-CCP), acute-phase reactants (ESR, CRP), and symptom duration. Score of 6 or more out of 10 classifies as definite RA.

lab_markers.value: Rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP antibodies, elevated ESR, elevated CRP, normocytic anemia, thrombocytosis

epidemiology.value: Prevalence approximately 0.5-1% worldwide. Female-to-male ratio 2-3:1. Peak onset between ages 40-60. Genetic association with HLA-DR4.

description.value: Chronic widespread pain disorder characterized by heightened pain sensitivity, fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive dysfunction without identifiable structural or inflammatory pathology.

symptoms.value: Widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, cognitive difficulties (fibro fog), sleep disturbance, headaches, irritable bowel symptoms, paresthesias, morning stiffness

diagnostic_criteria.value: 2016 revised ACR criteria: widespread pain index (WPI) score of 7 or more and symptom severity scale (SSS) score of 5 or more, OR WPI 4-6 with SSS 9 or more. Symptoms present at similar level for at least 3 months. No other disorder that would explain the pain.

lab_markers.value: No specific laboratory markers. All inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), autoantibodies (ANA, RF), and imaging are typically normal. Diagnosis is clinical.

epidemiology.value: Prevalence 2-4% of general population. Female-to-male ratio approximately 2:1. Can occur at any age but most commonly diagnosed in middle age.

description.value: Lupus-like syndrome triggered by specific medications, typically resolving after drug discontinuation. Shares features with SLE but rarely involves renal or CNS disease.

symptoms.value: Joint pain, myalgia, fatigue, fever, serositis (pleuritis/pericarditis), skin rash (less commonly malar), weight loss

diagnostic_criteria.value: Temporal association with offending drug (typically months to years of exposure), positive ANA and anti-histone antibodies, lupus-like symptoms, and resolution of symptoms upon drug discontinuation. Common offending agents include hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, and minocycline.

lab_markers.value: Positive ANA, anti-histone antibodies (present in >95% of cases), normal complement levels (unlike SLE), anti-dsDNA usually negative

epidemiology.value: Incidence approximately 15,000-30,000 cases per year in the US. More common in older adults due to higher medication use. Male-to-female ratio more equal than SLE.

description.value: Chronic autoimmune disorder targeting exocrine glands, primarily salivary and lacrimal glands, causing dryness of the eyes and mouth with potential systemic manifestations.

symptoms.value: Dry eyes (keratoconjunctivitis sicca), dry mouth (xerostomia), fatigue, joint pain, parotid gland enlargement, dental caries, vaginal dryness, skin dryness

diagnostic_criteria.value: 2016 ACR/EULAR classification criteria requiring a score of 4 or more from weighted items: labial salivary gland biopsy with focal lymphocytic sialadenitis, anti-SSA/Ro positivity, ocular staining score, Schirmer test, and unstimulated salivary flow rate.

lab_markers.value: Anti-SSA/Ro antibodies, anti-SSB/La antibodies, positive ANA, elevated ESR, rheumatoid factor, hypergammaglobulinemia, low complement

epidemiology.value: Prevalence 0.5-1% of general population. Female-to-male ratio approximately 9:1. Peak onset between ages 40-60. Can occur as primary disease or secondary to other autoimmune conditions.

description.value: Overlap syndrome combining features of SLE, systemic sclerosis, polymyositis, and rheumatoid arthritis, defined by the presence of high-titer anti-U1 RNP antibodies.

symptoms.value: Raynaud phenomenon, swollen hands (puffy fingers), joint pain, myositis, fatigue, sclerodactyly, esophageal dysmotility, pulmonary hypertension

diagnostic_criteria.value: Alarcon-Segovia criteria: positive anti-U1 RNP at high titer (>1:1600) plus at least 3 of 5 clinical criteria: hand edema, synovitis, myositis, Raynaud phenomenon, and acrosclerosis. Features evolve over time.

lab_markers.value: High-titer anti-U1 RNP antibodies, positive ANA (speckled pattern), elevated CK (if myositis), elevated ESR, mild cytopenias possible

epidemiology.value: Rare, with estimated prevalence of 3.8 per 100,000. Female-to-male ratio approximately 16:1. Typical onset between ages 20-40.

description.value: Fatal viral encephalitis caused by Lyssavirus, transmitted through the bite of an infected animal. Nearly 100% fatal once clinical symptoms appear without post-exposure prophylaxis.

symptoms.value: Prodromal phase: fever, malaise, paresthesia at bite site. Encephalitic phase: aggression, hydrophobia, aerophobia, hypersalivation, confusion, hallucinations, autonomic instability. Paralytic phase: ascending paralysis, coma

diagnostic_criteria.value: Clinical suspicion based on animal bite history and progressive encephalitis. Confirmed by direct fluorescent antibody test on nuchal skin biopsy, rabies virus RNA by RT-PCR in saliva or CSF, or rabies-specific antibodies in serum or CSF of unvaccinated patient.

lab_markers.value: Rabies virus RNA (RT-PCR in saliva/CSF), direct fluorescent antibody test (DFA) on skin biopsy, rabies virus-specific antibodies, CSF pleocytosis with elevated protein

epidemiology.value: Approximately 59,000 deaths worldwide per year, primarily in Asia and Africa. Extremely rare in developed countries due to animal vaccination programs. Incubation period typically 1-3 months.

description.value: Rapidly progressive and fatal prion disease causing spongiform encephalopathy. Characterized by rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, and distinctive EEG and MRI findings.

symptoms.value: Rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, visual disturbances, cerebellar ataxia, behavioral changes, pyramidal and extrapyramidal signs, akinetic mutism in terminal stages

diagnostic_criteria.value: WHO criteria: progressive dementia plus at least two of myoclonus, visual/cerebellar symptoms, pyramidal/extrapyramidal features, or akinetic mutism. Supported by periodic sharp-wave complexes on EEG, 14-3-3 protein in CSF, or characteristic MRI (cortical ribboning, caudate/putamen signal).

lab_markers.value: 14-3-3 protein in CSF, RT-QuIC assay (high sensitivity and specificity), total tau in CSF, periodic sharp-wave complexes on EEG, cortical ribboning on MRI DWI

epidemiology.value: Incidence approximately 1-2 per million population per year worldwide. Sporadic form accounts for 85% of cases. Median age of onset 60-65 years. Median survival 5 months.

description.value: Autoimmune encephalitis caused by antibodies against the GluN1 subunit of the NMDA receptor, leading to progressive neuropsychiatric symptoms, seizures, movement disorders, and autonomic instability.

symptoms.value: Psychiatric symptoms (psychosis, paranoia, agitation), seizures, movement disorders (orofacial dyskinesias, choreoathetosis), decreased consciousness, autonomic instability, hypoventilation

diagnostic_criteria.value: Definitive diagnosis requires presence of anti-NMDA receptor antibodies (IgG) in CSF. Clinical criteria include rapid onset of at least 4 of 6 symptom groups: abnormal behavior/cognition, speech dysfunction, seizures, movement disorder, decreased consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction or central hypoventilation.

lab_markers.value: Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies (IgG) in CSF, CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis, EEG showing extreme delta brush pattern, MRI may be normal or show T2/FLAIR signal changes

epidemiology.value: Most common form of autoimmune encephalitis. Predominantly affects young women (median age 21). Associated with ovarian teratoma in approximately 40% of female cases. Incidence estimated at 1.5 per million per year.

description.value: Acute necrotizing encephalitis caused by herpes simplex virus (primarily HSV-1 in adults), with predilection for temporal lobes. Most common cause of sporadic fatal encephalitis.

symptoms.value: Acute fever, headache, altered consciousness, behavioral changes, seizures, aphasia, focal neurological deficits, olfactory hallucinations, temporal lobe seizures

diagnostic_criteria.value: Clinical suspicion in acute encephalitis with temporal lobe involvement. Confirmed by HSV PCR in CSF (sensitivity >95%). MRI showing T2/FLAIR hyperintensity with hemorrhagic changes in temporal lobes is highly suggestive.

lab_markers.value: HSV PCR in CSF, CSF pleocytosis with lymphocytic predominance, elevated CSF protein, red blood cells in CSF (hemorrhagic necrosis), MRI with temporal lobe T2 changes and hemorrhage

epidemiology.value: Incidence approximately 2-4 per million per year. Bimodal age distribution with peaks in young adults and elderly. Without treatment, mortality exceeds 70%. With acyclovir, mortality reduced to 15-20%.

description.value: Novel neurotropic RNA agent-driven encephalopathy, transmitted via bite vector from infected animals. Characterized by a 3-phase clinical progression from prodromal mood changes through neuropsychiatric deterioration to persistent altered consciousness with necrotic lesions.

symptoms.value: Prodromal: fatigue, irritability, insomnia, bloodshot eyes. Neuropsychiatric: escalating aggression, confusion, hypersalivation, severe photophobia, hydrophobia, violent outbursts, compulsive biting and cannibalistic behavior directed at other humans. Motor: severe ataxia progressing to shuffling uncoordinated gait (shambling ambulation), progressive loss of speech replaced by involuntary groaning vocalizations. Sensory: profound analgesia with complete absence of pain response. Dermatologic: progressive skin necrosis with grayish pallor, sunken periorbital features, tissue decomposition at bite site. Behavioral: paradoxical approach behavior toward uninfected individuals despite diminished cognitive function. Animal bite history with unidentified species.

diagnostic_criteria.value: CDC classification criteria: animal bite exposure history, progressive neuropsychiatric syndrome not explained by standard infectious or prion workups, novel CSF protein bands on electrophoresis, novel 2-3Hz periodic EEG pattern, and identification of novel viral-like particles on electron microscopy of CSF or tissue specimens.

lab_markers.value: Novel CSF protein bands on electrophoresis, 2-3Hz periodic generalized EEG discharges (distinct from CJD pattern), novel viral-like particles on electron microscopy (80-100nm icosahedral), negative standard infectious panel (rabies, HSV, West Nile, HIV), negative prion markers (14-3-3, RT-QuIC)

epidemiology.value: Extremely rare. As of 2025, only 3 confirmed cases in North America, all linked to unidentified animal bite exposure in wilderness or rural settings. Suspected bite vector transmission with high pathogen titers in saliva. No known effective treatment.

description.value: Chronic metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and progressive beta-cell dysfunction, leading to hyperglycemia and increased risk of cardiovascular, renal, neurological, and ophthalmologic complications.

symptoms.value: Polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia, fatigue, blurred vision, slow wound healing, recurrent infections, acanthosis nigricans, peripheral neuropathy

diagnostic_criteria.value: ADA criteria: fasting plasma glucose 126 mg/dL or more, 2-hour plasma glucose 200 mg/dL or more during OGTT, HbA1c 6.5% or more, or random plasma glucose 200 mg/dL or more with classic symptoms. Requires confirmation with a second test on a different day unless unequivocal hyperglycemia is present.

lab_markers.value: HbA1c, fasting glucose, 2-hour OGTT, fructosamine, C-peptide, fasting insulin, lipid panel, serum creatinine, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio

epidemiology.value: Global prevalence approximately 6.1% (537 million adults). Rapidly increasing, especially in low- and middle-income countries. Strong association with obesity, sedentary lifestyle, and family history. Risk increases with age.

description.value: Progressive inflammatory lung disease causing obstructed airflow from the lungs, encompassing chronic bronchitis and emphysema. Primarily caused by long-term exposure to irritating gases or particulate matter.

symptoms.value: Chronic cough, sputum production, dyspnea on exertion, wheezing, chest tightness, frequent respiratory infections, fatigue, weight loss in advanced disease, barrel chest

diagnostic_criteria.value: GOLD criteria: post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC ratio less than 0.70 on spirometry in a patient with appropriate symptoms and risk factor exposure. Severity graded by FEV1 percentage predicted (GOLD 1-4).

lab_markers.value: Spirometry (FEV1, FVC, FEV1/FVC ratio), arterial blood gas, CBC (polycythemia in chronic hypoxia), alpha-1 antitrypsin level, chest X-ray, CT chest

epidemiology.value: Global prevalence approximately 10% in adults over 40. Third leading cause of death worldwide. Primary risk factor is tobacco smoking (80-90% of cases). Occupational exposures and air pollution contribute.

description.value: Persistently elevated arterial blood pressure without identifiable secondary cause. Major risk factor for cardiovascular disease, stroke, chronic kidney disease, and heart failure.

symptoms.value: Usually asymptomatic (silent killer). Severe hypertension may cause headache, visual changes, chest pain, dyspnea, epistaxis, dizziness

diagnostic_criteria.value: ACC/AHA 2017 criteria: sustained blood pressure of 130/80 mmHg or higher on two or more readings on two or more occasions. Stage 1: 130-139/80-89. Stage 2: 140/90 or higher. Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring may be used for confirmation.

lab_markers.value: Basic metabolic panel (creatinine, potassium, sodium), urinalysis, lipid panel, fasting glucose, ECG, echocardiogram (for target organ assessment), urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio

epidemiology.value: Affects approximately 1.28 billion adults worldwide. Prevalence increases with age, reaching over 60% in adults over 60. More prevalent in African American populations. Leading modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease.

description.value: Most common form of anemia worldwide, caused by insufficient iron to support normal red blood cell production. Results from inadequate dietary intake, impaired absorption, or chronic blood loss.

symptoms.value: Fatigue, pallor, dyspnea on exertion, palpitations, dizziness, pica (craving non-food items), koilonychia (spoon nails), angular cheilitis, glossitis, restless legs

diagnostic_criteria.value: Low hemoglobin (below 12 g/dL in women, 13 g/dL in men) with microcytic hypochromic red cell indices (low MCV, low MCH), low serum ferritin (below 30 ng/mL), low serum iron, elevated TIBC, and low transferrin saturation (below 20%). Reticulocyte count is low.

lab_markers.value: CBC with indices (low MCV, low MCH), serum ferritin, serum iron, TIBC, transferrin saturation, reticulocyte count, peripheral blood smear, soluble transferrin receptor

epidemiology.value: Affects approximately 1.2 billion people globally. Most common in premenopausal women, pregnant women, young children, and populations in developing countries. Leading cause of disability-adjusted life years lost.

description.value: Self-limiting inflammation of the bronchial airways, usually caused by viral infection. Characterized by cough lasting up to 3 weeks, often following an upper respiratory infection.

symptoms.value: Cough (productive or dry), low-grade fever, chest discomfort, fatigue, mild dyspnea, sore throat, rhinorrhea, myalgia

diagnostic_criteria.value: Clinical diagnosis based on acute cough lasting up to 3 weeks without evidence of pneumonia (no focal consolidation on exam or chest X-ray). Absence of chronic lung disease. Chest X-ray should be performed if pneumonia is suspected (high fever, tachycardia, hypoxia, focal lung findings).

lab_markers.value: Generally no specific lab tests needed. CBC may show mild leukocytosis. Procalcitonin can help distinguish bacterial from viral infection. Chest X-ray to exclude pneumonia when clinically indicated.

epidemiology.value: One of the most common outpatient diagnoses. Annual incidence approximately 5% of adults. More common in fall and winter months. Over 90% of cases are viral in etiology. Accounts for significant antibiotic overprescribing.
